      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 19.6.0: Thu May  6 00:48:39 PDT 2021; root:xnu-6153.141.33~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.5
      npm: 6.14.14
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.17.5

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

MacOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Other platform

### Describe the Bug

## Version 11

NextJS 11 had the `serverless` target which, despite some more dependencies than advertised, was an attempt at making  serverless functions from each dynamic page. The idea was that the serverless page could be deployed like a render function only without any resources. This was in fact almost right, even though some dependencies actually existed, which was reported here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/26297. Nonetheless, despite these extra resources, you could actually copy the render function along with about five or so dependency chunks and it all worked; you didn't even have to run a server to render the response.

Note well that NextJS adds some middleware to both API requests and API responses:

* https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/api-middlewares
* https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/response-helpers

An arbitrary API page written for NextJS will assume that these exist. In NextJS version 11, one of the dependency chunks that I talked about, that resides inside the `.next/serverless/pages/chunks` folder, enhances the `req` and `res` objects with this in function `apiResolver`.

To sum up, in version 11, all you needed was the following file system to serve a given page:

    .next
        serverless
            chunks
                 ... (about 5 chunks)
            pages                 
                api
                    ... (api pages)
                ... (regular pages)
    index.js (some file that reads an incoming message and then calls resources in `.next`)

## Version 12
Version 12 has ditched the `serverless` target at the benefit of [outputting a file for each page containing information about the dependencies](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing). This way we will clearly see the needed dependencies and may copy them to the location where they're needed. There is also the option to let NextJS do this for us, whereby a `standalone` folder with `node_modules` and the whole shebang is included. The problem now, no matter if we use the standalone option or not, the `apiResolver` method where the extra middleware is added has been moved to `node_modules/next/server/api-utils.js` which means we MUST include `node_modules` folder to get the expected NextJS equipped `req` and `res` objects in our standalone pages. It also seems that the `apiResolver` method, which was in version 11 called from the standalone api function itself, is now called from the `base-server` in the server package, indicating that we need to start a server to ornament the `req` and `res` objects with the necessary api middleware / helpers.

## Conundrum
It seems to me that from a general serverless perspective, NextJS just took a step backwards and we are with version 12 required to both keep a `node_modules` folder and also start a Node / Next server to be able to properly handle api requests whereas we could do the same in version 11 without both. Starting a server is more time consuming than just rendering the response from a function and keeping the `node_modules` folder around takes up more space than necessary as well. Perhaps given some specific serverless platform, this is a step in the right direction but if so, then it is a shame that it should be at the cost of a general usability in serverless contexts.

Please enlighten me if I have misunderstood something or if I'm wrong in any way, I would be really glad if I am :)

### Expected Behavior

Improved architecture from version 11, perhaps with the rendering functions actually being standalone as advertised instead of depending on the `.next/serverless/chunks` folder.

### To Reproduce

1. Start a new Next project with `npx create-next-app`
2. Run `next build`
3. Copy the contents of `.next` folder to a new folder `serverless`
4. Verify the dependencies for default api `hello` endpoint in `.next/server/pages/api/hello.js.nft.json` to be only the main `package.json` and `.next/server/webpack-api-runtime.js`
5. Delete all files in the `serverless` folder except `.next/server/webpack-api-runtime.js` and `.next/server/pages/api/hello.js`
6. At the root, create a serverless entrypoint `index.js` with content

        const renderer = require("./serverless/server/pages/api/hello")
        const http = require("http")

        const run = () => {
            const req = new http.IncomingMessage(null)
            const res = new http.ServerResponse(req)
            return renderer.default(req, res)
        }

        run()

7. Run `node index.js`
8. Verify error `res.status is not a function`

The default api endpoint makes use of NextJS specific `res.status()` ad `res.json()` which doesn't work since the `req` and `res` objects have not been ornamented with these extra capabilities through the `apiResolver` function.

Adding 

    module.exports = {
        experimental: {
            outputStandalone: true,
        }
    }

to `next.config.js` and using the entire `.next/standalone` in place of the above `serverless` folder does not change the results, indicating that we must indeed spawn a server (perhaps the included `server.js`) to get the embellished `req` and `res` objects that are expected, which in turn also suggests that we need to keep a `node_modules` folder around.

title: When Jest is used with coverage enabled, it reports incorrect uncovered lines
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up) Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.2.0: Sun Nov 28 20:28:54 PST 2021; root:xnu-8019.61.5~1/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 16.13.2 npm: 8.1.2 Yarn: N/A pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.0.8-canary.22 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ### What version of Next.js are you using? Tested with 12.0.7 and 12.0.8-canary.22 ### What version of Node.js are you using? Test with 14.18.3 and 16.13.2 ### What browser are you using? FIrefox (not sure this is relevant for this) ### What operating system are you using? macOS Monterey 12.1 ### How are you deploying your application? Vercel ### Describe the Bug When following Next.js documentation to use Jest and when the "--coverage" flag is added to Jest, it reports incorrect "Uncovered Lines" which makes it difficult to attain target coverage. ```js // pages/index.js import Head from 'next/head' import Image from 'next/image' import styles from '../styles/Home.module.css' export default function Home() { return (
Create Next App

Welcome to Next.js!

Get started by editing{' '} pages/index.js

Documentation →

Find in-depth information about Next.js features and API.

Learn →

Learn about Next.js in an interactive course with quizzes!

Examples →

Discover and deploy boilerplate example Next.js projects.

Deploy →

Instantly deploy your Next.js site to a public URL with Vercel.

Powered by{' '}
) } ``` There is nothing additional to test for line 11 here. ### Expected Behavior I would expect Jest coverage to report only lines that need additional tests to be covered. ### To Reproduce * Use `npx create-next-app@latest` as indicated here: https://nextjs.org/docs/getting-started#setup * Configure Jest with the Rust compiler (https://nextjs.org/docs/testing#setting-up-jest-with-the-rust-compiler): * `npm install --save-dev jest @testing-library/react @testing-library/jest-dom` * Create a jest.config.js file with this content: ```js const nextJest = require('next/jest') const createJestConfig = nextJest({ // Provide the path to your Next.js app to load next.config.js and .env files in your test environment dir: './', }) // Add any custom config to be passed to Jest const customJestConfig = { setupFilesAfterEnv: ['/jest.setup.js'], // if using TypeScript with a baseUrl set to the root directory then you need the below for alias' to work moduleDirectories: ['node_modules', '/'], testEnvironment: 'jest-environment-jsdom', } // createJestConfig is exported this way to ensure that next/jest can load the Next.js config which is async module.exports = createJestConfig(customJestConfig) ``` * Add the Jest executable with coverage to the package.json scripts (`"test": "jest --coverage"`) (Reference: https://nextjs.org/docs/testing#creating-your-tests) * At this point, running `npm run test` produces this error message: ``` Validation Error: Module /jest.setup.js in the setupFilesAfterEnv option was not found. is: /Users/davidlaganiere/Development/nextjs-test Configuration Documentation: https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration ``` To resolve this problem, I added a `jest.setup.js` file with the content shown in the `with-jest` example here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-jest/jest.setup.js * Per https://nextjs.org/docs/testing#creating-your-tests, create the `__tests__` folder and add a test `index.test.jsx` ```js import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react' import Home from '../pages/index' describe('Home', () => { it('renders a heading', () => { render() const heading = screen.getByRole('heading', { name: /welcome to next\.js!/i, }) expect(heading).toBeInTheDocument() }) }) ``` * Use `npm run test` to run the test and see line `11` in the `Uncovered Lines` section even though this line is already covered by the existing test. As a side note, I first noticed the problem with a very slighly more complex example compared to this setup here and in my case, unexisting lines are shown. For example, I see lines `38-48` but the file being tested only has 36 lines. If you would like to try the issue with this setup, you can find it at https://github.com/davidlag0/todo-nextjs I have been trying to fix this issue and understand what causes it for a few hours but I don't have enough experience to find the cause by myself. If this is related to a configuration mistake on my part, to a bug with Jest or something else, then I hope I will learn something new here!

Chrome

macOS

locally

### Describe the Bug

When calling an API route with a locale, NextJS is returning a 404.  

I've used the example 'i18n-routing'  NextJS provides - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/i18n-routing and added an API route.  I used the first example here -  https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/dynamic-api-routes

When I try and access - http://localhost:3000/api/post/233. I get a successful response.
However accessing this with a locale, it throws a 404 - http://localhost:3000/fr/api/post/233

Interestingly, I downgrade the example to use "next": "10.2.1" and everything works. E.g:

http://localhost:3000/api/post/233 - OK	
http://localhost:3000/fr/api/post/233 - Ok


### Expected Behavior

When calling an API route with a locale I expect the API to be called and not throw a 404.

### To Reproduce

I've used the example 'i18n-routing'  NextJS provides - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/i18n-routing and added an API route `pages/api/post/[pid].js`. I added this example:

```
export default function handler(req, res) {
    const { pid } = req.query
    res.end(`Post: ${pid}`)
  }
```

When I call `http://localhost:3000/api/post/233` it's successful
When I call `http://localhost:3000/fr/api/post/233` it's fails with 404

title: Next.js 12.0.8 breaks "this" in TS classes
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

Operating System:
       Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #51~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Fri Jan 7 06:51:40 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.0.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.2

### What browser are you using?

Chrome, Safari, Firefox

### What operating system are you using?

Android, IOS, macOS, Windows, Linux

### How are you deploying your application?

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

After upgrading Next.js from 12.0.7 to 12.0.8, an important system broke at our company.
TypeScript code that had classes like the example below broke, because `this` became `undefined`.
(check out "To Reproduce" section)

### Expected Behavior

I would expect `this` in a method to actually refer to the instance of the class and not be `undefined`.
(as also specified in [TS docs](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/classes.html#arrow-functions)) 

This code has been in the codebase for quite a while and has survived major Next.js releases, until this minor one :)

### To Reproduce

```
// example.ts
export default class Example {
  readonly status: string;

  constructor({ status }: { status: string }) {
    this.status = status;
  }

  public activate = async (data: string) => {
    // Some code...
    this.status // WHOOPS! Exception thrown - this undefined! (worked fine in 12.0.7)
  };
}
```

```
// index.ts
const example = new Example({ status: "GOOD" })
example.activate("some string");
```

title: CSS Modules sometimes don't load when using next/dynamic import
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.3.0: Thu Jan 21 00:06:51 PST 2021; root:xnu-7195.81.3~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.0
      npm: 6.14.13
      Yarn: 1.22.10
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.17

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

When loading a component using `next/dynamic`, CSS modules are sometimes not loaded correctly. This seems like it may be similar to https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/18252, which is described as fixed (although there is no linked PR showing the fix). 

This bug only occurs when running `next build` + `next start`. Works as expected in development  (`next dev`). Additionally, there seem to very specific scenarios where it occurs, which I've tried to outline as clearly and concisely as possible int he repro steps.

### Expected Behavior

CSS modules should be loaded into the document so that your styles are applied.

### To Reproduce

## Repro Repo and Deployment

I've created a demo using `create-next-app --typescript` 12.0.8: https://github.com/jzxhuang/dynamic-import-css-module-next-bug

It's hosted at: https://dynamic-import-css-module-next-bug.vercel.app/

## Isolated Reproduction Steps

1. Go _directly_ to https://dynamic-import-css-module-next-bug.vercel.app/broken/one, notice the button has a red background. 
2. Click the link to `/foo`. The button will now have a clear background, even though it is the exact same component with no changes whatsoever.

This can be reproduced with both SCSS and CSS modules. Repeating the above steps with https://dynamic-import-css-module-next-bug.vercel.app/broken/two will break the blue button. This is reproduced _only_ in the "production" build, if you follow the same steps with `next dev`, the buttons on `/foo` should always be the correct color.

## Cases where it works, but why?

The above steps are an isolated reproduction of this bug which we found in production. Now, here's where it starts getting super weird, we decided to do some more experimentation. I'm not sure if the following scenarios will be helpful or just throw you into a wild goose chase, but here we go:

1. Go to https://dynamic-import-css-module-next-bug.vercel.app
2. Click the link to `/broken/one`, DO NOT REFRESH
3. Click the link to `/foo`.
4. It's expected that the button is now red... 

We also created a few other examples, under the routes `/working/*`. The only difference here is that we either load 2, 3, or 0 of the buttons. For some reason, these seems to work fine, but if you load only _one_ button, as shown in `broken/one` and `broken/two`, the styling will be broken.

## If you don't lazy load, everything is fine

Back to something that makes sense. The example `/working/three` shows that the blue button will always be the correct color. It is not dynamically imported in /foo, and thus does not have the same bug that the dynamically imported buttons do. 

## What's going on in the DOM?

On /broken/one, we see the button has the class `comp-one_button__MtA0_` which is in the stylesheet `0ff09bacf4af1553.css`.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26701004/149401388-db140d90-f9cf-4975-9b31-83dab530fc59.png)

When we click into `/foo`, the button still has the same class, but the stylesheet is not being applied.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26701004/149401586-4aa58c10-80eb-4f09-80d2-40c368379945.png)

I'm not sure what the root cause of this might be, but there's somehow a stylesheet missing when dynamically importing, and it seems to be related to using a component on the _previous_ component inside the dynamically imported component.

title: Using spread props in a `next/head` child causes void elements to be decorated with a `children` prop
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.1
      npm: 8.1.3
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.18.1

### What browser are you using?

Microsoft Edge Version 97.0.1072.55

### What operating system are you using?

Windows 11

### How are you deploying your application?

N/A

### Describe the Bug

When using spread props (`{ ...props }`) on a child of `next/head`, the element is decorated with a `children` prop set to `[ undefined, false, undefined, undefined ]`. When this occurs on a void element, this causes an unrecoverable build error.

```
Server Error
Error: link is a void element tag and must neither have `children` nor use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`.
```

This is what ReactDOM is passed, and tries to validate:

```
{
  tag: 'link',
  props: {
    href: '/favicon.svg',
    sizes: '192x192',
    rel: 'icon',
    children: [ undefined, false, undefined, undefined ]
  }
}
```

[In the reproduction](https://github.com/dstaley/next-head-rest/blob/main/pages/index.js#L21), you can see that no `children` prop is being passed to the element.

### Expected Behavior

Page builds correctly and renders in the browser.

### To Reproduce

[Reproduction Repo](https://github.com/dstaley/next-head-rest)

After cloning the repo and installing dependencies, run `npm run dev` and visit `http://localhost:3000` in a browser.
author: dstaley

title: 500 Error - "static responses cannot be piped. This is a bug in Next.js"
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

_No response_

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.7

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.15.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

MacOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Custom server/Other platform

### Describe the Bug

Experiencing intermittent 500s in production across all SSG pages with revalidation. When checking server logs, found this errror:
```
Error: invariant: static responses cannot be piped. This is a bug in Next.js
at RenderResult.pipe (/var/app/node_modules/next/dist/server/render-result.js:18:19)
at Object.sendRenderResult (/var/app/node_modules/next/dist/server/send-payload.js:58:22)
at runMicrotasks (<anonymous>)
at processTicksAndRejections (internal/process/task_queues.js:95:5)
```
Looking a little deeper into the render-result file it appears that the result might be an empty string.

Also the 500 errors reported from users are blank with the text "Internal Server Error" and not our custom error page.

### Expected Behavior

Expect a successfully rendered page or the custom error page.

### To Reproduce

Unfortunately, haven't been able to reproduce this issue due to it's inconsistency and can't seem to find any pattern other than all the 500s are from SSG pages.

### Describe the Bug

I'm using Grommet (with Styled Components) to attempt to center an element. I'm actually copying over some old code that I had from plain React, which featured a centered input form. But for some reason when I attempt to center it, it works on saving the source, but whenever I refresh the page, the styles immediately revert back to before I applied the styles. The styles are then permanently not applied on the component.

### Box Component with centered items
```js
<Box flex align='center' width='large'>
   { props.children }
</Box>
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43776043/149503101-f16ac30f-28d2-4cda-bcd9-3819f779b23e.png)

Putting `alignSelf` and `alignContent` as well as `justify` produces the same result of being centered, then going back to not having the styles applied. It keeps going back to the top left corner of the screen on page refresh.

As mentioned above I have gotten this to work in plain React as I've written a much older version of this app over there.

### Expected Behavior

I was thinking it would center the form permanently, not just upon saving the source, then reverting after a refresh. Here's the plain React version:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/43776043/149504854-7b085c3d-9085-4d85-9959-fae91af156f0.png)


### To Reproduce

- By using Styled Components, align the component to the center, then save the changes. 
- Then refresh the page and it will return to the top left corner of the page.

### _app.js
```js
function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <Provider>
      <Grommet full theme={ yellowJacket }>
        <DashboardTemplate>
          <Component {...pageProps} />
        </DashboardTemplate>
      </Grommet>
    </Provider>
  )
}

export default MyApp
```

### Template.jsx
```js
export default function DashboardTemplate(props) {
    //const [currentUser] = useAtom(userAtom)

    return ( 
        <Box height='100%' fill background='light-1'>
            {/*
                currentUser ?
                <Topbar /> :
                false
            */}
            {/* <Topbar /> */}
            <Box
                flex='grow'
                direction='row'
                overflow={{
                    horizontal: 'hidden'
                }}
            >
                {/*
                    currentUser ?
                    <Sidebar /> :
                    false
                */}
                {/* <Sidebar /> */}
                <Box flex align='center' justify='center' width='large'>
                    { props.children }
                </Box>
            </Box>
        </Box>
    )
}
```

### index.js (login form)
```js
export async function getServerSideProps(context) {
  return {
    props: {
      currentUser: null,
      sidebarToggle: true
    }
  }
}

export default function Home({ currentUser, sidebarToggle }) {
  useHydrateAtoms([
    [userAtom, currentUser],
    [sidebarAtom, sidebarToggle]
  ])

  return (
    <Box align='center'>

              name='username'

              type='text'
              required
            />
          </FormField>
          <FormField margin={{ top: 'medium' }}>
            <TextInput
              name='password'
              placeholder='Password'
              type='password'
              required
            />
          </FormField>
          <Button
            primary

title: Client bundles contain non es5 code
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP PREEMPT Wed, 05 Jan 2022 16:20:59 +0000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.7.0
      npm: 7.20.3
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.7.0

### What browser are you using?

Chromium, Firefox

### What operating system are you using?

Linux

### How are you deploying your application?

next start

### Describe the Bug

The update to 12.0.8 introduced a regression w.r.t. es5 compatibility. If running the command series given below the following error occurs:

```
ES-Check Error:
          ----
          · erroring file: ./.next/static/chunks/main-1e7421553b9673ee.js
          · error: SyntaxError: The keyword 'const' is reserved (1:97874)
          · see the printed err.stack below for context
          ----

          SyntaxError: The keyword 'const' is reserved (1:97874)
```

I think this is related to the changes in #33159 which added a new method `getProperError` to the `ìs-error.js` file. This method is used on the client side in `next/client/index.tsx`. The previously used `isError` function was very simple and es5 compatible. The new method uses the `isPlainObject` method which contains a `const` declaration.

I think the root cause is that `dist/lib` isn't transpiled during building. Looking at the [Babel loader regex](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L472-L477) only `dist/shared/lib` seems to be transpiled. So moving those files there might be a solution for this problem.

### Expected Behavior

The Webpack bundle files contain only es5 compatible JavaScript code.

### To Reproduce

- npx create-next-app@latest --typescript
- npx next build
- npx es-check es5 './.next/static/**/*.js'
author: tfkhim

title: Implement getServerSideProps for the _app page to allow data from the server to be loaded into a template wrapper.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Feature request: Implement getServerSideProps for the _app page to allow data from the server to be loaded into layout components on each page load. This would be great for implementing a dynamic menu system that automatically generates from the /pages directory structure.

### Describe the solution you'd like

As above. I need some way of transferring data from my server to my _app template on each page load.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

My motivation: I want to create a dynamic nav menu based on my /pages directory to scan the folder and update menu links automatically as I add new files. I am trying this in stages.

Stage 1: I have created a standalone navbar page with its own getServerSideProps to read the file system and return /pages. This works fine.

Stage 2: I want this to load on every page but _app doesnt support its own getServerSideProps and Next js wont call getServerSideProps on my Navbar if I import it as part of my wrapper template into _apps.

Is there a way to create a shared component that loads with _app and can also read from the server?

body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)


    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.1.0: Wed Oct 13 17:33:23 PDT 2021; root:xnu-8019.41.5~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:

      next: 12.0.9-canary.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome, Safari

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Other platform

### Describe the Bug

12.0.7 does't have this bug, Only 12.0.8 (and canary)

Running "next build" command I received this error:

```
> Build error occurred
Error: Cannot find module 'browserslist-config-company'
    at Function.webpackEmptyContext [as resolve] 
```

The package is properly installed:
![Schermata 2022-01-14 alle 23 37 09](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6173088/149594266-8e5ab3ad-cfd7-4bbf-9f79-f01b640e1bfa.png)

The package.json browserlist:

```
"browserslist": [
    "extends browserslist-config-company"
  ],
```

Definition of _browserslist-config-company/index.js_

`module.exports = ['cover 90% in IT', 'last 2 years', 'not dead', 'not IE 11'];`

Including browserlist rules from an external file ("_extends browserslist-config-company_"), probably is the root cause of the problem. Hard coding the rules:

```
"browserslist": [
    "cover 90% in IT", "last 2 years", "not dead", "not IE 11"
  ],
```
all works as expected 





### Expected Behavior

Including browserlist rules from an external package, it won't break the build.

### To Reproduce

Simply, try to define in your package.json the browserslist attribute, including rules from an external package

title: Dynamic import in _app is included in main bundle and bundle size becomes unnecessarily large.
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.2.0: Sun Nov 28 20:28:54 PST 2021; root:xnu-8019.61.5~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.1
      npm: 6.14.15
      Yarn: 1.19.1
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

14.18.1

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

darwin

### How are you deploying your application?

vercel

### Describe the Bug

We are trying to load framer-motion features after the page becomes interactive because animations should be a progressive enhancement and aren't required for the page to function.

Dynamic imports seem to be included in main bundle when more things are imported:
https://stackblitz.com/edit/nextjs-rx15fu?file=pages%2F_app.tsx

### Situation 1: LazyMotion is in the `_app.tsx` but not using framer-motion

[Treemap](https://googlechrome.github.io/lighthouse/treemap/?gzip=1#)

- We see a small `pages/_app` bundle (not even shown by default)
- We see a small `pages/index` bundle (not even shown by default)
- We see the framer-motion chunk (chunks/22 in this case)

### Situation 2: LazyMotion in `_app.tsx` and `m` used in `index.tsx`

[Treemap](https://googlechrome.github.io/lighthouse/treemap/?gzip=1#)


- We see a small `pages/_app` bundle (not even shown by default)
- We see an increate for `pages/index` bundle, but expected
- We see the framer-motion bundle now being split up in two parts. Not sure why this is happening, but that probably is the shared part between the projectin bundle and the `m` component?

So far so good

### Situation 3: LazyMotion in `_app.tsx` AND `m` used in `_app.tsx`

[Treemap](https://googlechrome.github.io/lighthouse/treemap/?gzip=1#)


- `pages/index` is back to it's initial size, which is expected, because those parts are loaded in `_app`.
- The `pages/_app.tsx` bundle has gobbled up everything, now including the common parts but more importantly al the framer-motion stuff that needed to be loaded later.

🤯

Any ideas?

### Expected Behavior

I would expect the main chunk of the bundle to always be an external file as it isn't supposed to be preloaded in this case.

### To Reproduce

Steps to generate the treemaps:

1. Download the stackblitz example
2. npm i
3. npm run build && npm run start
4. Run lighthouse
5. Open the Treemap viewer

Or maybe even simpler:

App should be smaller than 92.7kb:

```
/_app                                  0 B            92.7 kB
```
author: paales

title: Docs: conflict in next/script's onLoad documentation
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

I'm afraid there's currently a conflict between the two documentation pages on next/script. The [Script Component page says](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/script#executing-code-after-loading-onload) following:

> If you are loading a script with either `beforeInteractive` or `afterInteractive` as a loading strategy, you can execute code after it has loaded using the `onLoad` property

However, in the [API reference page on `next/script`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/script#onload), under `onLoad`, it says:

> Note: `onLoad` can't be used with the `beforeInteractive` loading strategy.
 
As far as I know, the `onLoad` prop for `beforeInteractive` is not currently working and there's [an open issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33191) on that. Either way, the documentation should be in line with the current state of affairs.

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

I can see the reason why a separate API documentation page for next/script exists in addition to the guide page on script optimization, but I would hope that any time either of those two are updated, the other would be checked for inconsistencies.

The inconsistency was created in #33097.

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

_No response_

14.17.3

N/A

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

next start

### Describe the Bug

When using a pnpm workspace alongside the new next `swc` compiler, the dev server fails to start on macOS when using a `pnpm` workspace where the next.js application is in a package in a subdirectory. This seems to be due to webpack not being able to load the `amp-dev.js` client entrypoint located in the hoisted `node_modules` folder in the root of the workspace. It also seems to not be able to load the `next-dev.js` entrypoint, but the `amp-dev.js` entrypoint fails first.

This bug only occurs when a `.babelrc` file is NOT present. When using babel, the server starts normally.

### Expected Behavior

The development server should start normally.

### To Reproduce

Github repo with minimal reproduction: https://github.com/mgreenw/next-pnpm-swc-bug

This repo was bootstrapped with `npx create-next-app@latest --typescript`, and then I deleted the `yarn.lock` file, moved the next.js package to `packages/next`, added a `pnpm-workpsace.yaml` file, and then ran `pnpm install`.

1. Clone the repo above
2. Run `pnpm --filter next dev`
3. You should see:
![Screen Shot 2022-01-17 at 1 42 19 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2615374/149823478-1ac94588-7d28-4bfc-b2ca-925ba9cbc33e.png)

title: Key prop must appear first in JSX when using spread attributes (using react-table, 12.0.8 Regression)
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

### My computer
    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Fri Apr 2 22:23:49 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### Codesandbox example I created
    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #52-Ubuntu SMP Thu Sep 10 10:58:49 UTC 2020
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.1
      npm: 6.14.15
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.9-canary.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.1

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

Windows (Ubuntu WSL) and Ubuntu

### How are you deploying your application?

next build

### Describe the Bug

In 12.0.8 and using [React-Table](https://github.com/tannerlinsley/react-table), I noticed a regression where attribute/prop placement matters for a key when combined with a spread attribute is being used. If the key prop comes after the spread attributes, the top-level map element will contain no children. If the key prop comes before the spread attributes, then everything works as expected.

### Expected Behavior

The expected behaviour is the order of the key prop when combined with spread attributes not to affect the render.

### To Reproduce

https://codesandbox.io/s/bold-mountain-e896n

In the CodeSandbox, there are two react tables. The only difference between the two tables is the prop placement, if the key prop/attribute goes in front or behind the spread attributes. The first table has the key prop in front of the spread attributes while the second table is vice-versa. I should expect to see two tables side-by-side but instead I see one table and another table (that is visibly hidden) with only empty fields.

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

17.2.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Other

### Describe the Bug

I've written some middleware using the new API to rewrite urls for fully translated routing within my app. I had it all working with a custom express server using version `12.0.5-canary.11`.

I then subsequently upgraded to `12.0.7` and now get this error:

```
Error: To use middleware you must provide a `hostname` and `port` to the Next.js Server
```

This check was introduced in [`12.0.5-canary.18` ](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/31935/commits/a29a1fd624f17e92943c00b787fae2d1eaae52ce)

This problem also occurs with the most basic Middleware implementation:

```tsx
import type { NextRequest, NextFetchEvent } from 'next/server';
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server';

export function middleware(req: NextRequest, _ev: NextFetchEvent) {
  return NextResponse.next();
}
```



### Expected Behavior

Should be able to use middleware with custom server, with no error

### To Reproduce

Use a the express custom server with the basic middleware implementation
author: ost-ing

title: Feature Request: `next build` should log all TSC errors, not just the first one
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Currently, `next build` fails on the first TSC error and prints only that error. For instance, if you have `N` TypeScript errors in your project that will prevent `next build` from succeeding, you will run `next build` `N` times, revealing them one at a time, unless you manually run `tsc --noEmit` or something.

Next Server should collect all of the errors emitted by TSC and log all of them, not just throw on the first one and log it.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Implement change above. Collect all errors from TSC and log them all, rather than just the first one.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Run `tsc --noEmit`, resolve errors, and then run `next build`.

title: Differentiate between SSG and fallback: 'blocking'
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Use case:
Im using getStaticPaths to generate the most popular pages, and the rest can be generated on the fly with fallback blocking

I've added some tasks that take some time to run (about 5-6s)  in getStaticProps (generated blurDataUrl for remote images etc). This is fine for the pre-generated pages returned by getStaticPaths however for the other pages it takes a long time for the user.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A boolean isFallbackBlocking in GetStaticPropsContext would be the solution, which would allow to skip the time-consuming tasks on fallback.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Current workaround is just to not do time-consuming tasks in getStaticProps, even if they are pre-rendered

      react: 18.0.0-rc.0

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.1

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

MacOS

### How are you deploying your application?

Doesn't matter

### Describe the Bug

I wanted to try out the `concurrentFeatures` flag with React 18 in our application, but am faced with the error `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'publicRuntimeConfig')` on the server side. This is locally with `next dev`.

So for some reason calling `getConfig()` (from `next/config`) returns undefined when the `concurrentFeatures` flag is turned on. On the client `getConfig()` returns `{ publicRuntimeConfig: {}, serverRuntimeConfig: {} }` (always empty objects).

_**Side note:** This leaves the browser request hanging. No 500 or anything. In the server logs there are also no reference to where the code is used. No stack trace with line numbers etc. Just the bare message. This could probably be an issue on its own, but leaving it here for now._



### Expected Behavior

getConfig() should include all config set up in `next.config.js`

### To Reproduce

* Bootstrap a new application
* Set up some publicRuntimeConfig in `next.config.js`
* Upgrade to React 18 RC and turn experimental flag `concurrentFeatures` on
* Try to access publicRuntimeConfig in a page by calling `getConfig().publicRuntimeConfig`

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

I'm trying to use the library `image-blob-reduce` with next.js, and it works fine in the dev mode (`next dev`), but it crashes on usage in the prod build (`next start`).

[`image-blob-reduce` is an ES Module](https://github.com/nodeca/image-blob-reduce/blob/master/dist/image-blob-reduce.esm.mjs), that contains the `pica` library which is built with `browserify`. `pica` also uses [`webworkify`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/webworkify) to complicate things.

Some more investigation info can be found here: https://github.com/nodeca/pica/issues/228

### Expected Behavior

This should work:

```
import ImageBlobReduce from "image-blob-reduce"

// ...
// in an event handler:
new ImageBlobReduce().toBlob(file, { max: 100 }).then((blob) => {
    console.log("converted image successfully", blob)
})
```

### To Reproduce

The error can be reproduced when deploying this codesandbox to Vercel:

https://codesandbox.io/s/image-blob-reduce-next-js-mqj7i?file=/pages/index.js


On the page, open the console and select a file with the input. This error shows up:
```
Uncaught ReferenceError: n is not defined
    at t (4f00a94f-75f9-4f8e-864e-3a077e53aef3:1:314)

(anonymous) @ 4f00a94f-75f9-4f8e-864e-3a077e53aef3:1
```

In dev mode in Codesandbox it works correctly.
author: fabb

title: Modifying mongoose schemas not populating until development server is restarted
body:
### What example does this report relate to?

with-mongodb-mongoose

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8 

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.4.2

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

docker-compose

### Describe the Bug

Modifying schemas (e.g adding a required field) don't populate to entire app unless you restart the development server (i.e.  npm run dev). 

### Expected Behavior

Modifying schemas should populate just like other pages/components without the need to restart development server. 

### To Reproduce

Clone the `with-mongodb-mongoose-app' example:
```bash
npx create-next-app --example with-mongodb-mongoose with-mongodb-mongoose-app
# or
yarn create next-app --example with-mongodb-mongoose with-mongodb-mongoose-app
```

Initialize the setup:
1. add your mongodb uri to `.env.local`
2. `npm i` or `yarn install`

Start the development server:
`npm run dev` or `yarn dev`

Trigger mongoose create the Pet schema by making a POST request to `localhost:3000/api/pets`

curl example:

`
curl --request POST \
  --url http://localhost:3000/api/pets \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{
	"name": "x",
	"owner_name": "me",
	"species": "dog",
	"image_url": "any link address"
}'
`

Go to `/models/Pet.js` and modify the Pet schema; for example try these two scenarios:

1. add a required field named 'blah'
2. remove the required 'name' field

```js

// adding the 'blah' required field
const PetSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  blah: {
    type: String,
    required: [true, 'Please provide a blah for this pet.'],
    maxlength: [20, 'Blah cannot be more than 60 characters'],
  },
  // name: {
  //   /* The name of this pet */
  //   type: String,
  //   required: [true, 'Please provide a name for this pet.'],
  //   maxlength: [20, 'Name cannot be more than 60 characters'],
  // },
// rest of the original code below
...})

```

Save the file and now try to insert a new record by making a POST request to `/api/pets/index.js`. This time ommit `name` field and add `blah` field. It's expected to receive no errors as the `name` field is not required now and we are passing the new-required `blah` field.

curl example: 
`
curl --request POST \
  --url http://localhost:3000/api/pets \
  --header 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{
	"blah": "x",
	"owner_name": "me",
	"species": "dog",
	"image_url": "any link address"
}'
`

You will get a 400 response at this stage. console.logging the error message: 

`
error Error: Pet validation failed: name: Please provide a name for this pet.
`

This indicates that our modifications to the Pet schema is not yet populated to app.

restart the development server and try again. This time you will receive a success response, indicating that our changes have populated.

### Describe the Bug

Using spread props on intermediate component causes its child components to be re-mounted on each re-render.

To reproduce,

3. Go to `http://localhost:3000/spread`, change the input, you'll find that every input change will cause a full remount on the input element.

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

Windows with WSL2 (Ubuntu)

### How are you deploying your application?

next start

### Describe the Bug

We recently switched our larger next application to yarn 3 with pnp. When we run `next build` or `next dev`, we get a lot of errors about missing node packages.

A few examples:

> (node:8335) [MODULE_NOT_FOUND] Error: randombytes tried to access process. While this module is usually interpreted as a Node builtin, your resolver is running inside a non-Node resolution context where such builtins are ignored. Since process isn't otherwise declared in randombytes's dependencies, this makes the require call ambiguous and unsound.
> (Use `node --trace-warnings ...` to show where the warning was created)
> (node:8335) [MODULE_NOT_FOUND] Error: @sentry/utils tried to access process. While this module is usually interpreted as a Node builtin, your resolver is running inside a non-Node resolution context where such builtins are ignored. Since process isn't otherwise declared in @sentry/utils's dependencies, this makes the require call ambiguous and unsound.
> (node:8335) [MODULE_NOT_FOUND] Error: safe-buffer tried to access buffer. While this module is usually interpreted as a Node builtin, your resolver is running inside a non-Node resolution context where such builtins are ignored. Since buffer isn't otherwise declared in safe-buffer's dependencies, this makes the require call ambiguous and unsound.
> (node:8335) [MODULE_NOT_FOUND] Error: @contentful/rich-text-from-markdown tried to access util. While this module is usually interpreted as a Node builtin, your resolver is running inside a non-Node resolution context where such builtins are ignored. Since util isn't otherwise declared in @contentful/rich-text-from-markdown's dependencies, this makes the require call ambiguous and unsound.

What I tried:

- In next.config.js, set a fallback for the packages:
```
webpack: (config) => {
    config.resolve.fallback = {
        util: false,
        process: false,
        path: false
      };
```
This doesn't seem to do anything.
- In next.config.js, set an alias for the packages:
```
webpack: (config) => {
    config.resolve.alias= {
        util: false,
        process: false,
        path: false
      };
```
This breaks the build completely.
- Try the things above, but inside a `if(!isServer)` block -> same result.
- Try experimental flags:
```
experimental: {
    swcFileReading: false,
    esmExternals: false,
    workerThreads: true
  },
```

### Expected Behavior

No errors should appear in the build.

### To Reproduce

I don't have a reproducible example yet as the codebase is private. The problem seems to be with multiple dependencies. If necessary to resolve this I could spend some time to figure out how to reproduce this in a smaller repo.

author: idrisakmal

title: Cookie maxAge handling is incorrect or undocumented in Next.js NextResponse
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

_No response_

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.7

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.8.0

### What browser are you using?

Edge (Chromium)

### What operating system are you using?

Windows 11

### How are you deploying your application?

On Azure App Service as docker container

### Describe the Bug

In the [NextResponse documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/server#public-methods) example it looks like the cookie maxAge is set in milliseconds (maxAge: 1000 * 60 * 60 * 24 * 7).

It's very easy to make a mistake here (like I did) because typically the [Max-Age in Set-Cookie is expressed in seconds](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie).

I took a look at the [code](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/1d4f364515d34586b864923dafb7888bf1ff79d2/packages/next/server/web/spec-extension/response.ts#L52) and it looks like internally the value gets divided by 1000 to get back seconds. But the fact that milliseconds is expected in the first place is not documented. Please clarify.

Potentially related: 
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/27617
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/31666

I also want to point out that [nookies](https://github.com/maticzav/nookies) (a Next.js cookie library) expects seconds, which makes it confusing in projects that use both NextResponse and nookies.

### Expected Behavior

If I set maxAge when defining a cookie with NextResponse I expect it to be in seconds, not milliseconds. Or at least properly document this difference.

### To Reproduce

We set cookies in a [Next.js middleware](https://nextjs.org/docs/middleware).

```typescript
  response.cookie(experimentsCookieName, JSON.stringify(newExperimentInfo), {
    path: '/',
    maxAge:  4 * 60 * 60, // I intended this cookie to have a 4 hour expiration, but it has a 14.4 seconds expiration instead!
  });
```
author: zmarty

title: Allow setting middleware rewrite destination on an existing NextResponse
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

TL;DR: Please create a helper function which attaches the x-middleware-rewrite header to an existing NextResponse that we previously created with NextResponse.next()

We use [Next.js middleware](https://nextjs.org/docs/middleware) for A/B testing - we send users to one variation of a page or another.

To determine which page (A or B) to serve to a user:
1) We check if a browserId is stored in a cookie called browserId. If not, we generate a GUID with no expiration and set it as the value of the browserId cookie for subsequent visits.
2) Once we know the browserId, we send this semi-permanent browserId to an internal A/B testing endpoint that decides whether to place this user on the A side or B side of an experiment.
3) Rewrite the location to go to either A.tsx or B.tsx page.

Notice that in step 1 we do not know if we are sending the user to page A or B. Also, in this step 1 we sometimes need to **set** a cookie with the browserId. To do that, we need call NextResponse.next() to get an empty response, then we call response.cookie('browserId', ...) on it. 

In steps 2 and 3 we determine where to send the user and at this point we need to call [rewrite](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/d7062dddcc4f7e679cf52214e2a9fc0d87e4f068/packages/next/server/web/spec-extension/response.ts#L89) with the correct path. However, since we started with an empty response that we want to modify, we cannot call NextResponse.rewrite(...) anymore because that would generate a separate new response. What we do instead is a bit of a hack, we manually set the _x-middleware-rewrite_ header on the response we created for step 1.

Since this header name _x-middleware-rewrite_ can presumably change in the future, I propose having an official helper function.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Please create a public helper function which attaches the x-middleware-rewrite header to an existing NextResponse that we previously created with NextResponse.next(). Then have the rewrite function internally call this same helper function.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We can postpone setting the cookie described in step 1 after we performed steps 2 and 3 but this would be ugly and it would not follow normal logic.

title: Error with comment in SVG URI in CSS file
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Aug 25 23:20:18 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.2

### What browser are you using?

N/A

### What operating system are you using?

Ubuntu (WSL)

### How are you deploying your application?

npm run build

### Describe the Bug

In Next.js v12, webpack has troubles when importing a CSS file that has a comment in SVG URI. Note that this previously worked fine in Next.js v11.

This is the error message.

```
Module build failed: UnhandledSchemeError: Reading from "data:image/svg+xml;charset=UTF-8,<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<!./-- Generator: Adobe Illustrator 24.0.1, SVG Export Plug-In . SVG Version: 6.00 Build 0)  -->
<svg version="1.1" id="dx_l_3" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" x="0px" y="0px"
         width="24px" height="24px" viewBox="0 0 24 24" style="enable-background:new 0 0 24 24;" xml:space="preserve">
<path style="fill:" is not handled by plugins (Unhandled scheme).
Webpack supports "data:" and "file:" URIs by default.
You may need an additional plugin to handle "data:" URIs.
    at /home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:49033:25
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:135839:10), <anonymous>:16:1)
    at Hook.CALL_ASYNC_DELEGATE [as _callAsync] (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:135641:14)
    at Object.processResource (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:49030:8)
    at processResource (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:134812:11)
    at iteratePitchingLoaders (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:134763:10)
    at runLoaders (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:134989:2)
    at NormalModule._doBuild (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:49020:3)
    at NormalModule.build (/home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:49164:15)
    at /home/addison/Node.js/nextjs-temp/fix-css-svg-bug/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/webpack/bundle5.js:23636:12
```

Below is an snippet of CSS that causes the bug. If you add this to a CSS file, Next.js will crash when attempting to build (i.e. `npm run build`).

```css
.dx-list:not(.dx-list-select-decorator-enabled) .dx-list-item.dx-state-hover .dx-icon-toggle-delete {
  background-image: url("data:image/svg+xml;charset=UTF-8,%3C%3Fxml%20version%3D%221.0%22%20encoding%3D%22utf-8%22%3F%3E%0A%3C!--%20Generator%3A%20Adobe%20Illustrator%2024.0.1%2C%20SVG%20Export%20Plug-In%20.%20SVG%20Version%3A%206.00%20Build%200)%20%20--%3E%0A%3Csvg%20version%3D%221.1%22%20id%3D%22dx_l_3%22%20xmlns%3D%22http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F2000%2Fsvg%22%20xmlns%3Axlink%3D%22http%3A%2F%2Fwww.w3.org%2F1999%2Fxlink%22%20x%3D%220px%22%20y%3D%220px%22%0A%09%20width%3D%2224px%22%20height%3D%2224px%22%20viewBox%3D%220%200%2024%2024%22%20style%3D%22enable-background%3Anew%200%200%2024%2024%3B%22%20xml%3Aspace%3D%22preserve%22%3E%0A%3Cpath%20style%3D%22fill%3A%23333%3B%22%20d%3D%22M12%2C2C6.5%2C2%2C2%2C6.5%2C2%2C12c0%2C5.5%2C4.5%2C10%2C10%2C10s10-4.5%2C10-10C22%2C6.5%2C17.5%2C2%2C12%2C2z%20M18%2C13H6v-2h12V13z%22%2F%3E%0A%3C%2Fsvg%3E%0A");
}
```

This is the URL-decoded SVG file:

```svg
<!-- Generator: Adobe Illustrator 24.0.1, SVG Export Plug-In . SVG Version: 6.00 Build 0)  -->
<svg version="1.1" id="dx_l_3" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" x="0px" y="0px"
         width="24px" height="24px" viewBox="0 0 24 24" style="enable-background:new 0 0 24 24;" xml:space="preserve">
<path style="fill:#333;" d="M12,2C6.5,2,2,6.5,2,12c0,5.5,4.5,10,10,10s10-4.5,10-10C22,6.5,17.5,2,12,2z M18,13H6v-2h12V13z"/>
</svg>
```

This bug is identical to the one posted here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30721. The issue was closed as being fixed but that's incorrect. The issue is still present in the latest Next.js (even canary, I've tried).

## What's the bug

I've figured out what's causing the problem, I just don't know how it's supposed to work, so maybe someone can enlighten me.

The problem is that the CSS snippet above has a `!` inside it (even though it's URL encoded). In the SVG, it has a comment which is denoted by `<!--`, which is what's causing the issue.

The bug occurs in `postcss-url-parser` which is responsible for parsing the `url()` function in CSS files. See here: https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/blob/c5062db3fc849d882a07b9f2c9f66f00325c8896/src/plugins/postcss-url-parser.js#L253-L258

The prefix is meant to be used in Webpack to specify a specific module when importing, so I presume that's why the logic is present. I know this is the problem because when I remove it locally, the build error goes away.

This code was added March 24th, 2021 in webpack-contrib/css-loader (source: https://github.com/webpack-contrib/css-loader/commit/263427eef1f0e3a460fe95ed4e0613197d1ba8d9). No message was given as to why it was added. This code was then copied locally to Next.js last summer I believe.

### To Reproduce

Add the CSS snippet provided above to your Next.js project and it will have this issue. Just make sure the CSS file is imported in the source code somewhere.

title: next/image does not work with CDN
body: ### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up) Operating System: Platform: win32 Arch: x64 Version: Windows 10 Pro Binaries: Node: 16.13.0 npm: 8.1.0 Yarn: N/A pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.0.8 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ### What version of Next.js are you using? 12.0.8 ### What version of Node.js are you using? 16.3.0 ### What browser are you using? Chrome ### What operating system are you using? Windows, Linux ### How are you deploying your application? Other platform ### Describe the Bug The issue relates to a [previous bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/20968), but that case was closed. However, I believe the issue still persists. We have the CDN URL specified in the `assetPrefix`: // next.config.js module.exports = { assetPrefix: 'https://cdn.example.com' } Following the guide from the [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/cdn-support-with-asset-prefix): > Files in the public folder; if you want to serve those assets over a CDN, you'll have to introduce the prefix yourself In the code we have the following `Image` tag: But the result in the markup is like this: ### Expected Behavior The expected behaviour should be that the initiator URL (`src="/_next/image?url...`) should also have the CDN prefix, like `src="https://cdn.example.com/_next/image?url...`. All compiled assets are loaded fine from the CDN by the way... so the `_next` folder is served from the CDN. That proves the `assetPrefix` setting is working properly, only the `Image` is failing. ### To Reproduce Use both `` and `assetPrefix`. Load image from the `public` folder.

body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up) _No response_ ### What version of Next.js are you using? 11.1.2 ### What version of Node.js are you using? v14.18.3 ### What browser are you using? Chrome ### What operating system are you using? Ubuntu ### How are you deploying your application? Other platform ### Describe the Bug Hello guys, on my app I have several pages that uses some HOCs to access context as props, like this one: **`ComponentPage.jsx:`** ``` import withSomeContext from 'utils/withSomeContext' const ComponentPage = ({states, actions) => { return (...) } ComponentPage.getLayout = function getLayout(page) { return (
Some Header
{page}
) } export default withSomeContext(ComponentPage) ``` But then when I pass some props like `getLayout` I can't really retrieve it on the `_app` because of that withSomeContext HOC: **`_app.js:`** ``` function MyApp({Component, pageProps}) { console.log(Component.getLayout, 'I can't retrieve my getLayout prop') ---> undefined return ( <...> (...) ) } export default MyApp ``` This happens because the `Component` prop on the `_app` sees is my `withSomeContext` HOC function instead of my real Page Component. Do you guys can think on any workaround for being able to actually retrieve these kind of props we set for a Page Component while using a HOC to render it? Am I missing something? **`withSomeContext.js:`** ``` import {SomeContextConsumer} from 'contexts/SomeContextConsumer' export const withProperties = (Component) => (props) => { return ( ) } ``` ### Expected Behavior Be able to retrieve Page Component props (e.g. getLayout) while using a HOC. ### To Reproduce Having a page component that is exported using a HOC.

### Describe the Bug

I have a lambda function handler like this, and I want to bundle it to serve the bundled version over the wire:

```javascript
const server = require('restana')();
const app = require('next')({ dev: false });
const files = require('serve-static');

const bodyParser = require('body-parser');

const nextRequestHandler = app.getRequestHandler();

server.use(files(path.join(__dirname, 'build')));
server.use(files(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));

server.all('/api/*', bodyParser.json(), (req, res) => nextRequestHandler(req, res));

server.all('*', (req, res) => nextRequestHandler(req, res));

module.exports.handler = require('serverless-http')(server);
```

I am bundling this with webpack:

```javascript
const ZipPlugin = require("zip-webpack-plugin")

}

const webpackConfig = Object.assign(config, pluginConfig)
module.exports = webpackConfig

title: API Routes 204 > Conflict between TypeScript and linter rules
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

 Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.1.0: Wed Oct 13 17:33:24 PDT 2021; root:xnu-8019.41.5~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 7.24.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.2

### What browser are you using?

Edge

### What operating system are you using?

macOS 12.0.1

### How are you deploying your application?

localhost

### Describe the Bug

In a route API, if I want to return a 204 HTTP Code (No Content), I first added an empty object `{}` as body response, but I get an error inside my terminal :


> A body was attempted to be set with a 204 statusCode for /api/hello, this is invalid and the body was ignored.
> See more info here https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/invalid-api-status-body

So in the docs, we have this code example to use:
```js
export default function handler(req, res) {
  res.status(204).send()
}
```

But by using it, it's now a TypeScript which appears in VSC

```
Expected 1 arguments, but got 0.ts(2554)
utils.d.ts(217, 25): An argument for 'body' was not provided.
```

```ts
// utils.d.ts:217
/**
 * Send body of response
 */
declare type Send<T> = (body: T) => void;
```


I try to change the `res` type with `res: NextApiResponse<any>` but it's not change.
And if I use `res: NextApiResponse<void>`, the error disappear for the `204` return, but now is on a `200` or `400` for example.

The only solution looks like to force the cast as :
```ts
;(res as NextApiResponse<void>).status(204).send()
```

Is there any solution for casting `body` type depending of the status code?

Thanks

### Expected Behavior

Allow to use an empty/void parameter in the `send()` method for a 204 API response.

### To Reproduce

```ts
// /pages/api/hello.ts

import type { NextApiRequest, NextApiResponse } from 'next'

export default function handler(req: NextApiRequest, res: NextApiResponse) {
  res.status(204).send()
}
```

author: benallfree

title: RSC: loader improvement & shared components
body:
### Background

We have 2 webpack loders to handle the transform of server and client components on both server and client side, to make sure: 
* server components won't get executed on client side
* client components can be SSR’d

### Problem

#### Exports

Those loaders assume you define components with `export default` by default. Which has limited users' ability to export components more flexibly.

#### Shared components

And we don't know how the shared components are defined: if a component from a file with js extension is imported and can work well in both server/client components, we call them shared components. Loaders should be able to handle those cases to let shared components behave differently on the 2 sides.

Since the import paths could be aliased, the import identifiers could be renamed or assigned dynamically, it's hard to capture it as a shared one or not. This could be tricky.

title: Shallow Routing incorrectly recreate/remount component (lose state)
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

_No response_

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8 (also tried with 11.1.3)

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.5.0

### What browser are you using?

Chrome

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

next start, Netlify

### Describe the Bug

First shallow routing after redirect from another page will incorrectly remount component (any component state will be reset).
Subsequent shallow routing within the same page works as expected (keeping component state).



### Expected Behavior

Shallow routing within the same page should keep component state

### To Reproduce

- create 2 pages (i.e. page `A` and `B`)

- In page `B` component, add some local state
```typescript
const [value, setValue] = useState('initial');
```
-  From page A redirect to page B 
```typescript
router.push({ pathname: '/B/[slug], query: { slug: '1' } })
```

- In page B, update the local component state 
```typescript
setValue('updated');
```
and then do shallow routing to update the slug 
```typescript
router.push({ pathname: '/B/[slug], query: { slug: '2' } })
```
the state `value` will reset to `initial` (component recreated).

- Subsequent shallow routing works as expected and keep the state value

title: Custom server: Can't conditionally serve different Next directories
body:
### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.5.0: Sat May  8 05:10:33 PDT 2021; root:xnu-7195.121.3~9/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.0.8
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What version of Next.js are you using?

12.0.8

### What version of Node.js are you using?

16.13.2

### What browser are you using?

Safari

### What operating system are you using?

macOS

### How are you deploying your application?

node server.js

### Describe the Bug

I prepared a minimal repro and issue description here: [Next.js no conditional server issue (minimal repro)](https://github.com/just-learning-1/minimal-repros/tree/master/nextjs-no-conditional-server-issue)

### Goal

I want to conditionally serve different Next directories to different sessions within a custom Express server.

### Issue Summary

After Next app server serves one directory (`next({ dir: 'a' })`) in a server runtime, it seems impossible to later serve a different directory (`next({ dir: 'b' })`), even to an entirely different session or machine.

### Expected Behavior

### Expected behavior

(See code & repro) I expected to get served `dir: 'a'` when storing cookie value `'a'`, and to get served `dir: 'b'` when storing cookie value `'b'`.

Instead, after getting served either `'a'` or `'b'` once, all future attempts to conditionally serve will serve that first directory.

### To Reproduce

## Repro

1.  Clone the [minimal repro](https://github.com/just-learning-1/minimal-repros/tree/master/nextjs-no-conditional-server-issue)
2.  Run `yarn serve`
3.  Visit `localhost:4000` in a private browser window
4.  Read: `Visit /a or /b to continue`
5.  Visit `localhost:4000/a` (for this example)
6.  Read: `Your choice is a. Now, return to /`
7.  Visit `localhost:4000`
8.  Read: `Welcome to a`
9.  Now, visit `localhost:4000` in a different private browser window (or different machine)) and read instruction
10. Visit `localhost:4000/b` and read message
11. Finally, visit `localhost:4000`

**Expected:** Read: `Welcome to b`

**Actual:** Read: `Welcome to a`

*Note:* Visiting `/b` first will indeed persist `Welcome to b` instead.

## Code

Here's the code in `server.js`, minimally changed from the official [Custom Express Server example](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/custom-server-express):

```js
// server.js
const cookieParser = require('cookie-parser')
const express = require('express')
const next = require('next')

const port = parseInt(process.env.PORT, 10) || 4000
const dev = process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'

// Want different next apps & handlers to conditionally source different directories
const appA = next({ dev, dir: 'a' })
const handleA = appA.getRequestHandler()
const appB = next({ dev, dir: 'b' })
const handleB = appB.getRequestHandler()

const server = express()

server.use(cookieParser())

// Visit /a to receive cookie with choice=a
server.get('/a', (req, res) => {
  res.cookie('choice', 'a')
  res.status(200).send('Your choice is a. Now, return to /')
})

// Visit /b to receive cookie with choice=b
server.get('/b', (req, res) => {
  res.cookie('choice', 'b')
  res.status(200).send('Your choice is b. Now, return to /')
})

server.all('*', (req, res, goNext) => {
  const cookieChoice = req.cookies['choice'] || ''
  const chooseMessage = 'Visit /a or /b to continue'

  if (!cookieChoice) {
    // When no cookie, send message
    res.send(chooseMessage)
  } else {
    let app
    let handle
    switch (cookieChoice) {
      case 'a':
        // Set app & handler to serve directory 'a/'
        app = appA
        handle = handleA
        break
      case 'b':
        // Set app & handler to serve directory 'b/'
        app = appB
        handle = handleB
        break
      default:
        res.send(chooseMessage)
    }

    // Want to conditionally serve 'a/' or 'b/' here
    app.prepare()
      .then(() => {
        handle(req, res) 
      })

    goNext()
  }
})

server.listen(port, (err) => {
  if (err) throw err
  console.log(`> Ready on http://localhost:${port}`)
})
```

current Dockerfile: 
```Dockerfile
# Install dependencies only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS deps
# Check https://github.com/nodejs/docker-node/tree/b4117f9333da4138b03a546ec926ef50a31506c3#nodealpine to understand why libc6-compat might be needed.
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

COPY package.json yarn.lock ./
RUN yarn install --frozen-lockfile

# Rebuild the source code only when needed
FROM node:16-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN yarn build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM node:16-alpine AS runner

RUN adduser -S nextjs -u 1001

COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json

author: husseinhere

title: Should next.js handle the distribution of license information for open source dependencies?
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

After playing around with my create-next-app, I notice there is no builtin way to create and distribute the license files which are required to be available when using certain OSS. 

Generating such licenses is pretty easy with https://github.com/xz64/license-webpack-plugin (which NCC already uses). Given that information, I wonder if it makes sense to expose a "known route" to retrieve this information to make it easy to add a link to the notices.

In my opinion (and happy to discuss further) OSS compliance is exactly the "type" of web dev work that Next.js is equipped to totally handle and abstract away.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Perhaps use LicenseWebpackPlugin and expose the notice generated in some known way to be able to include it in the next.js documentation. Happy to help brainstorm further.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Alternatives require users manually creating and exposing license information, but this goes against "zero config" ideals.

The more common alternative is for users to just ignore these licenses and not provide any mechanism to attribute the OSS they are using. (I should note that I was also unable to find this information for vercel.com)

author: Tosinkoa

title: "Prop `href` did not match.", when using next/link from external package.
body: ### Run `next info` (available from version 12.0.8 and up) I don't think that works on codesandbox: `> No such directory exists as the project root: /sandbox/info`, sorry 😢 ### What version of Next.js are you using? 12.0.9 ### What version of Node.js are you using? 14.18.1 ### What browser are you using? Chrome ### What operating system are you using? macOS ### How are you deploying your application? any development mode / codesandbox ### Describe the Bug In short: I get a rehydration warning like this, when using next/link through any external package and use locales: ``` Warning: Prop `href` did not match. Server: "/my-page" Client: "/fr/my-page" ``` Where I think the problem is: When a i18n enabled next app uses `next/link` through an external package (that somehow enriches its behaviour), the environment variable `process.env.__NEXT_I18N_SUPPORT` is not defined (only on the server) within the `addLocale` function used by the link. Thus the link has no locale added on the server. On the client the locale attribute is present as expected. When react rehydrates the html it stumbles upon the a tag with mismatched href attribute. When not using dev mode the warning is suppressed but I have a hunch that the mismatch is still there. Also see the discussion here: #22130 (esp. some of my strange logging adventures [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/22130#issuecomment-833610774)) ### Expected Behavior `process.env.__NEXT_I18N_SUPPORT` should be defined within `next/link` no matter if server or client and no matter if imported directly or via an external package. Otherwise it would be impossible to write packages abstracting link related things. In other words using `next/link` through external packages should not cause a warning and should have correct html generated on the server. ### To Reproduce I have created a reproduction on **[Codesandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/nextjs-prop-href-did-not-match-9ob4o?file=/pages/index.js)**. Just check `pages/index.js` and the console. I published a package that does almost nothing but using `next/link` to create this reproduction (it only has `next` and `react` as peerDeps). Check the source here: https://unpkg.com/next-link-30d@1.0.0/index.js I would love if you could help me out. This bug has been haunting me for half a year now and I finally managed to reproduce it reliably. ❤️
