author: MauriceVanVeen

title: defaultLoaders.babel does not handle source maps from previous loaders in chain
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.3.0: Thu Jan 21 00:06:51 PST 2021; root:xnu-7195.81.3~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When putting `defaultLoaders.babel` as the last loader in a chain like so:

	webpack(config, options) {
		config.module.rules.push({
			test: /\.(coffee)$/,
			include: [options.dir],
			exclude: /node_modules/,
			use: [
				options.defaultLoaders.babel,
				{loader: 'coffee-loader'},
			],
		})

		return config
	},

`options.defaultLoaders.babel` doesn't handle the source maps passed by previous loaders (ie. in this case `coffee-loader`)

If switching to `babel-loader` instead the source maps passed by `coffee-loader` are being respected. (But obviously the niceties of default loader like fast refresh etc. are gone):

	webpack(config, options) {
		config.module.rules.push({
			test: /\.(coffee)$/,
			include: [options.dir],
			exclude: /node_modules/,
			use: [
				{loader: 'babel-loader'},  // <----------- this line changed
				{loader: 'coffee-loader'},
			],
		})

		return config
	},

### Expected Behavior

That `defaultLoaders.babel` should respect how webpack loaders are expected to work (supporting source maps even when chaining multiple loaders https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/531).

### To Reproduce

I created a minimal example with `npx create-next-app` at
https://github.com/Cottin/next-coffee-source-maps

0. `npm install`
1. `npm run dev`
2. See error popup on site in localhost:3000 pointing to `pages/index.coffee (1:0)`
3. Comment out `defaultLoaders.babel` in `next.config.js` and comment in `{loader: 'babel-loader'}` instead
4. See error popup on site in localhost:3000 pointing to `pages/index.coffee (6:2)` (ie. the correct line in the coffee file)

title: Docs: What is the proper way to build with NEXT_PUBLIC env vars in docker?
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

I have searched all over on how to get the `NEXT_PUBLIC` to take in my Dockerfile, but the resulting build does not have the value being used at all. 

### Is there any context that might help us understand?


In my frontend code, I am using:

```
const client = new GraphQLClient(`${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL}`, {
  method: 'GET',
})
```

And this works locally just fine when I use a `.env.local` file with the variable.

Things I've tried:

```
FROM node:16-alpine AS builder

ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL=${NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL}

... 

RUN echo "NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL=${NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL}" > .env
RUN npm i && npm run build
```

docker-compose:

```
  frontend:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: docker/Dockerfile.frontend
      args:
        - NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL=...
```

I've also made sure my `next.config.js` file has the following:

```
  publicRuntimeConfig: {
    NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL,
  },
```

*The built file will just have `NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL` as the value.*

```
Request URL: https://xyz.com/NEXT_PUBLIC_FRONTEND_API_URL
```

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/environment-variables#exposing-environment-variables-to-the-browser

### Describe the Bug

If I add a `<Head />` component in `_app.js`, `_document.js` and a page, the order of tags changed from next 12.0.10 to 12.1.0. I created an example repo.

## head order next@canary

v12.1.1-canary.4

```html
<meta content="_app.js" />
<meta content="index.js" />
<meta content="_document.js" />
```

## head order next 12.1.0

```html
<meta content="_app.js" />
<meta content="index.js" />
<meta content="_document.js" />
```

## head order next 12.0.10

```html
<meta content="_document.js" />
<meta content="_app.js" />
<meta content="index.js" />
```

### Expected Behavior

It's either a bug or this "feature" is not mentioned anywhere (release notes). In my opinion, the next 12.0.10 version is correct and this is a bug.

Next links should pass `href` by default. There's negative utility to removing this, and it's very useful for SEO and accessibility.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Setting `passHref` manually on every Next link.

title: Build on Next 12 takes over 30 minutes
body:
### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information ``` Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan 5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 14.17.4 npm: 7.23.0 Yarn: 1.22.17 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.0 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ``` ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next build ### Describe the Bug **TL;DR**: NextJS 12 build time increases drastically when CSS modules are added --- ## The story Long-time Next user here. Our main website runs entirely on NextJS and the experience has been pretty good so far. Lately we decided it was time to get our hands dirty and update from Next 10 to the new 12 with the SWC compiler and all that implies. Additionally, we decided to start using CSS modules instead of a single gigantic CSS file for the whole app made of a lot of imported, much smaller component styles. The goal is double-fold: improve developer experience and avoid serving unnecessary CSS code on each page to optimise network use. The overall migration process was rather easy. Next 12's development server uses a lot of RAM (min 2GB), but performance is really incredible, especially when hot reloading. However, our build time went from approximately 5 minutes to over 30 minutes. It has become so long in fact that it does not build properly on Heroku anymore. Here's one build log:

├ └ css/ea501070afc3c47b.css 17.3 kB ├ λ /tos 6.64 kB 703 kB ├ λ /travel 14.8 kB 878 kB ├ └ css/d7215ca0310291c6.css 8.97 kB ├ λ /travel/category/[category_id] 3.55 kB 875 kB ├ λ /travel/hotel/[hotel_id] 8.56 kB 907 kB ├ λ /travel/package/[id] 16.3 kB 823 kB ├ └ css/6f082bb6e2fdd2de.css 9.28 kB ├ λ /travel/search 4.73 kB 837 kB └ λ /unsubscribe 3.22 kB 694 kB + First Load JS shared by all 690 kB ├ chunks/framework-2e8d380ab91e9dce.js 46.5 kB ├ chunks/main-339de6c6280cb5e5.js 32.1 kB ├ chunks/pages/_app-596a641a25e82b41.js 608 kB ├ chunks/webpack-e8b2c8bcc23836a5.js 3.49 kB └ css/379a08765d2fe3bf.css 1.05 MB λ (Server) server-side renders at runtime (uses getInitialProps or getServerSideProps) Done in 2022.23s. -----> Caching build Saving 2 cacheDirectories (package.json): - node_modules - .next/cache -----> Pruning devDependencies yarn install v1.22.17 [1/4] Resolving packages... [2/4] Fetching packages... -----> Timed out running buildpack Node.js ! Push failed ```
As you can read near the last line of this log, it took **2,022.23** seconds to build the app oh Heroku —that's just a little bit shy of 34 minutes. On my local machine, with SSD and no CPU-intensive concurrent processes, it takes 1,241.31 seconds —approximately 21 minutes— to build the app after removing the `.next/` folder. I know that Heroku can cache some folders, and you can see that we configured the caching already to include `.next/cache`. However, no cache is available for most of our development branches builds because they are often short-lived, and need to be able to build and run a new branch without leveraging cache. ## The ideas and thoughts It seems the drastically increased build time stems from the CSS modules usage. You can guess from the build log that not all pages are currently leveraging CSS modules since not all of them have corresponding .css files. During development, it seemed that moving CSS code from the global stylesheet to a module increased build time as I went on. Now we're halfway there through the whole CSS refactoring process, and I'm unsure going through with it is a good idea because of this scalability issue. ## The questions Now I guess I have a few questions: 1. how can I profile the build process to understand what is taking the most time to build? 2. are there ways I do not know of that would help getting a faster build process? 3. is there a "soft-limit" regarding CSS modules count? ### Expected Behavior Build time should be similar if not improved over Next 10 or 11 ### To Reproduce - Use CSS modules (384 `*.modules.scss` files in our project currently) - `next build` If profiling the build process myself is too complicated, I guess I could share the project for investigation under an NDA. Unfortunately, it would take too much time for me to try and build using Next 12 without any CSS modules to be able to rule-out CSS modules. However, I strongly believe this is the cause of the increased build time here.

FYI I did a lot of work like this when I worked on Chrome DevRel's team. I'd be happy to volunteer to make this change.

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

I imagine that this blog post is less likely to be updated/maintained than the guide.

One thing that will need to be done beforehand is to review each page and make sure that all important ideas covered in the blog post are also covered in the guide. The main thing when deprecating/removing docs is to make sure no key ideas are lost.

https://nextjs.org/blog/forms

author: LeviticusNelson

title: Add support for query param forwarding in getStaticProps redirect
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

In our app, we have pages that use `getStaticProps`, ISR and `fallback: blocking`. The route param accepts a `slug` in the format of `[id]-[friendlyName]`.

If the user visits a URL with a correct `id`, but an incorrect `friendlyName`, we want to redirect them to the canonical URL. For example, a request comes in to `/event/123-my-first-event`. We find the event with ID `123`, but notice that the `friendlyName` in the database is actually `my-second-event`. Therefore the canonical URL is `/event/123-my-second-event`. In `getStaticProps` we perform a permanent redirect.

However, during these redirect all query parameters are lost. This is critical as analytics tracking params are frequently appended, and if the user gets redirected we lose all the tracking.

We would like the ability to optionally opt-in to forward the original request's query parameters onto the supplied `destination`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new option in redirect to be added, for example;

```js
return {
  redirect: {
    destination: '/event/123-my-second-event',
    permanent: true,
    forwardQueryParams: true
  }
}
```

When this is supplied, the Next router takes the `destination` and appends the query parameters from the original request. For example, a request to `/event/123-my-first-event?foo=bar` becomes `/event/123-my-second-event?foo=bar`

An implementation of this has been developed in a fork and is ready to be submitted as a PR if this idea is approved.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The only possible alternatives currently would be either;

1. Use `getServerSideProps`, but we lose the caching benefits of `getStaticProps`
2. Create a middleware in front of the page to check for the canonical URL in advance, however this would run on every request and essentially negate the caching benefits

body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.0
      npm: 8.5.2
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: 6.32.2
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.5
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

I have a package in my `node_modules` which uses an ESM package (`is-stream@^3.0.0`). The package using the ESM package is trying to `require` it which I know is an error and I have raised an issue in the downstream package [here](https://github.com/torusresearch/solana-embed/issues/31). This is fine as next offers a great option `experimental.esmExternals: 'loose'` in order to fix this issue. 

The bug that I have noticed is that so long as I have installed the package (in my example it's `@monsonjeremy/esm-external-package`) I don't even need to enable `experimental.esmExternals: 'loose'`, I am able to run the application just fine without getting this [error](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/import-esm-externals). If however I link to the package locally, then I get the error:
```txt
ESM packages (is-stream) need to be imported. Use 'import' to reference the package instead. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/import-esm-externals
```

I am really confused why linking has different behavior then installing the package from NPM and if this is expected.

### Expected Behavior

I should have uniform behavior when linking the package locally or installing it from NPM.

### To Reproduce

I have created an example repo here, which can be used to see the issue: https://github.com/monsonjeremy/esm-external-example-repo

Steps:
1. Clone the repo
2. Run `cd example-package`
3. Run `npm install && npm run build && npm link`
4. Now go to the next app directory `cd ../example-app`
5. Run `npm install`
6: Run `npm run dev`
7. See that the page is able to load as expected on `localhost:3000`
8. Stop the server and run `npm link @monsonjeremy/esm-external-package`
9. Restart the server with `npm run dev`
10. See that localhost:3000 now has an error: 
```txt
ESM packages (is-stream) need to be imported. Use 'import' to reference the package instead. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/import-esm-externals
```
author: monsonjeremy

title: NextJs Image won't load on Vercel (Error: Requested and resolved page mismatch:)

### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/49033195/156347487-4ab8cfd5-83be-4d01-9266-105edfb55a34.png)


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Brave

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

The image is loading on localhost but not loading on Vercel

### Expected Behavior

body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Aug 25 23:20:18 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.5
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

cURL

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

When using a middleware, POST requests to API-routes never resolve. Commenting out the code added to `next-server.ts` from vercel/next.js#34519 solves the issue. GET works as expected.

Apologies if this is a known issue, thought it would be good to bring up otherwise.

Sidenote: When setting `config.api.bodyParser: false` on API-routes, won't they still have been replaced in the middleware layer, or is it completely separate?

### Expected Behavior

POST requests to api-routes to work.

### To Reproduce

https://stackblitz.com/edit/nextjs-hhf3gk

author: lluzak

title: Image cache is not preserved between vercel deployments for images with absolute external src URL
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Happening at least on Next v12.1.0 and v12.1.1-canary.5 deployed to Vercel

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jan 12 22:22:45 PST 2022; root:xnu-7195.141.19~2/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.11.1
      npm: 8.0.0
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.5
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

Images optimized with next Image component and default cached on Vercel are not preserved when the `src` attribute is an external URL (i.e. images from images.unsplash.com). After each deployment, for those images, for the first request, the CDN will respond with a `x-vercel-cache: MISS` header.

In contrary this is not happening when an image has a relative `src` URL (i.e. from public folder). In this case the CDN will respond with HIT or STALE even after a deployment.

The image-optimization docs also state that images should be preserved between deployments (https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/image-optimization#usage). See note:
> Even if you redeploy, images cached in /_next/image / /_vercel/image will be preserved.

In other parts of the docs it is said that caches are purged on each deploy. But this seems not to be the case for images in `/_next/image` following the docs mentioned above and actually experiencing that images with a relative URL are actually cached between deployments.

As far as I understand the setup it should not make a difference where the original image comes from. Because the images are optimized and those newly created images are cached in the app.

### Expected Behavior

Image cache for images with absolute external src URL should be cached between deployments. The same way as it happens for images with a relative src URL.

### To Reproduce

I created a test next application which renders two images. One with a local image and one with an external image: https://github.com/rumtraubenuss/vercel-image-cdn-bug

Clone the repo and deploy it to vercel.

Open network dev tools. Make sure to check `Disable cache`. Open the deployed application. Follow the link on the index page. I had to put the images on a second page, otherwise the page preview image in the vercel UI would act as a cache warmer since it requests the actual deployment to render the preview image.

On the page `/cats` you will see two images. Inspect the dev tools network and have a look at the response headers for the two images: For the very first time you open the page for both images the CDN responds with a MISS. Subsequent request (reload the page) will get HIT or STALE responds for both images.

Deploy the app again (you can use the Redeploy option in the vercel UI) wait until it is deployed. Reload the application page. You will see that the local image will have a HIT or STALE, the one with the external URL will have a MISS and therefore take much longer time to render.

Locally I checked the folder `.next/cache/images` and it seems that both kind of images are written to it. So I don't understand why the external URL images are not preserved between deployments.

I tested this with several external images (not only from unsplash) and it was everytime the same behavior.
author: rumtraubenuss

title: Cannot resolve "fs/promises" error failing build
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #33-Ubuntu SMP Fri Feb 4 17:03:31 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.19.0
      npm: 8.5.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.32.2
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0 (also tested 12.1.1-canary.6)
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

not relevant

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

Since upgrading to Next.js 12.1.0 running `yarn build` produces this error for any import from `"fs/promises"` including pages, their imports and npm packages:

```
yarn run v1.22.17
$ node --version && next build
v14.19.0
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
Failed to compile.

./src/utils/server/getFileList.js
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs/promises' in '/home/mitsu/git/Projects/fgo-tools/src/utils/server'
```

This bug was particularly hard to pinpoint as these errors look exactly like the ones you would see when accidentally using Node.js 12 instead of 14 and only appear for `next build` and NOT `next dev`. 

I at first suspected Vercel to be using Node.js 12 and after asking on Twitter added `"engines": {"node":"^14"}` to my package.json (which just produced a warning about ^14 not matching 14.x, but oh well) and changed the build command to `node --version && next build` as seen above. This confirmed that the Vercel deployment was indeed using the exact same version as I was using locally, ruling out Vercel as the issue.

### Expected Behavior

The build succeeds as it did with Next.js 12.0.x and earlier versions.

### To Reproduce

- create a new Next.js project using Next.js version 12.1.0 or canary
- add `import { readFile } from "fs/promises";` to `./pages/index.js`
- run the `next build` command

You should get the following output:

```
$ next build
info  - Checking validity of types  
info  - Creating an optimized production build  
Failed to compile.

./pages/index.js
Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs/promises' in '/home/mitsu/git/Temp/reproduce-fs-import-crash/pages'


> Build failed because of webpack errors
```

--------

Edit: Wanted to clarify more clearly that this issue only appears on 12.1.0 and newer, not older versions. The main branch of my project is still on 12.0.x and deploys the same code with no issues.
author: Mitsunee

title: Add idx to router.object
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I'd like to propose adding [idx](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/a51803624638a626694b03c23b48e373eb586517/packages/next/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1410) to the [router.object](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#router-object).`idx` is included in the history state on every new history entry.

This is valuable for reporting purposes such as the number of page views in the current session. 

It's also valuable when building native-like applications using nextjs, particularly for back button behaviors. When replicating a native like experience, it's helpful to be able to check if a programatic `router.back()` would kick the user out of the app, and if so, fallback to another page (like a Homepage). This pattern frequently comes up for back buttons rendered on an error page and settings pages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new `idx` field (or perhaps `pageCount` / `pageIndex`?) added to the router object.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. Accessing `history.state.idx` directly. This works, but is undocumented and I'm concerned about the field changing or being removed in a next update and application code silently breaking.

2. Another option would be to include a `fallback` option with [router.back](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#routerback) so that users aren't backed out of the application.

title: with-iron-session SG Shows sensitive information before redirect
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

run `yarn create next-app --example with-iron-session with-iron-session-app`

(you can update next to canary and it does the same thing).

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Google Chrome 98.0.4758.102

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

yarn dev

### Describe the Bug

Shows the profile-sg page before redirecting to login page.

It's a split second, but that would be easy to copy data using a parser if it isn't completely dependent on the user being present. I also don't believe the answer is "Wrap the entire component's return in a `user.isLoggedIn` condition" - that's the entire point of using a hook like the example in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-iron-session/lib/useUser.ts. 

### Expected Behavior

redirects to login page without showing the page.

**Reason this is important:**

Example page: [slug].js

You will have a `getStaticProps` and `getStaticPaths` for the page, since (presumably) you will have data that you are fetching based on the slug. This means you cannot use `getServerSideProps` on said page - you must use the SG version. If your API cannot depend on session authentication (in my example, I am using Sanity CMS), it will actually pull and display the data before redirect.

The example only has publicly available data, but if you have sensitive information, this could lead to issues...

Is there any way to prevent this besides wrapping each component that needs authorization in [useUser](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-iron-session/lib/useUser.ts)? If so, can you please change the example so that you can prevent the page from loading _without_ a conditional close for `user.isLoggedIn` in each page/component that requires authorization? I'd really like to use this, but I'm going to have to find a different project until the example can be updated... I'm personally having other issues getting `useUser` to work in my project (it keeps redirecting whether the user is logged in or not for some reason), but this is a major deal breaker for me in general.

### To Reproduce

1. run `yarn create next-app --example with-iron-session with-iron-session-app`
2. `cd with-iron-session-app`
3. `yarn dev`
4. navigate to http://localhost:3000/profile-sg before logging in
author: AJax2012

title: Update the cache-control section of the documentation with Next 11+ defaults
body:
The Cache-Control header set by `next start` is correct, as it prevents accidental caching of responses that can't be cached by default. If the page uses SSR (getServerSideProps/getInitialProps) it will have the mentioned cache control header. If it's using SSG (getStaticProps or automatic static optimization) it will have `s-maxage=REVALIDATE_SECONDS, stale-while-revalidate` or if `revalidate` is not used `s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate`. If you want a different `Cache-Control` while using SSR you can use `res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'value_you_prefer')` and Next.js will keep it as-is in production. In development te `Cache-Control` header is overwritten to ensure every request is not cached.

_Originally posted by @timneutkens in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33204#issuecomment-1010840976_
author: TheHacker66

title: Consider format for custom image loader
author: timfuhrmann

title: Params should be configurable in next/image cloudinaryLoader
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

**The user should be able to dictate cloudinary params as per their requirements. If not provided, we can fallback to the default params.**

Currently, the user is only able to add `width` and `quality` as params to the `cloudinaryLoader`. For my use case, the cloudinary link should consist of other params for image size, for eg `t_web_ab_2x`. 

For example, People might not use `width` as attribute to specify image dimensions, they could other custom ways like `t_web_ab_4x` etc.

**Source Code reference:** https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/ed43c03ebb164d88657c933be569b499f8e1e074/packages/next/client/image.tsx#L813

```
// Current Implementation
function cloudinaryLoader({
  config,
  src,
  width,
  quality,
}: ImageLoaderProps): string {
  // Demo: https://res.cloudinary.com/demo/image/upload/w_300,c_limit,q_auto/turtles.jpg
  const params = ['f_auto', 'c_limit', 'w_' + width, 'q_' + (quality || 'auto')]
  const paramsString = params.join(',') + '/'
  return `${config.path}${paramsString}${normalizeSrc(src)}`
}
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

The params should be passed as argument to the cloudinaryLoader. We can have some default params if the user hasn't provided any.

Ideal implementation as per me:

```
function cloudinaryLoader({
  config,
  src,
  width,
  quality,
  params
}: ImageLoaderProps): string {
  // Demo: https://res.cloudinary.com/demo/image/upload/w_300,c_limit,q_auto/turtles.jpg
  const cloudinaryParams = params || ['f_auto', 'c_limit', 'w_' + width, 'q_' + (quality || 'auto')]
  const paramsString = cloudinaryParams.join(',') + '/'
  return `${config.path}${paramsString}${normalizeSrc(src)}`
}
```

where
```
params: ['f_auto', 't_web_ab_2x']
```
author: neeraj3029

title: Docs: Problem with Dockerfile on `with-docker` example - bug for npm users
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

A fresh project `with-docker` gives me an error when trying to build the Docker image for the first time.
The problem was due to the fact that I initialized the project via `npx` as suggested by the documentation (and at the same time do not have yarn installed on my system): the command used npm to install everything. As a result when trying to build the image it doesn't found a `yarn.lock` file and the building process return me an error.

I solved it by putting a wildcard on the COPY command:
```
COPY package*.json yarn*.lock ./
```

I can create the pull request myself if its good for you.
Already have the patch in my fork: https://github.com/fsgreco/next.js/commit/305d7eea1775c52bf33856e4e4f8dfb31dad6608

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

There are also some improvements that can be made on the documentation file about that example:
- The Dockerfile is multi-stage but the command suggested to build everything is a unique command
This means that the resulting image will be only the last one (the `production` one).
This could be solved by specifying that the build command should have a specific `--target` option.
I can try to write the fix for this if you agree. :)
In that case should I open a different pull request? 

Sorry for my caution, this is my first issue/pull request on this project :)

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://github.com/fsgreco/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-docker/README.md

title: (nextjs 12.1.0) When build with 'swcMinify: true', the 'global' variable will be empty in some libraries
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.2
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.5
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome Version 98.0.4758.102

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

* Our project utilizes tui-calendar@^1.15.3
* We decided to upgrade nextjs to `12.1.0` by following the instruction. And the main reason for doing this is the `standalone` output which largely reduces docker image size.
* Run `yarn start`
* When running page reaches code generated from `tui-calendar`, it crashes with error which is originated from line `18105` at `node_modules/tui-calendar/dist/tui-calendar.js`. Code at that line is:
```
this.guideElement = global.document.createElement('div');
```
* The reason is simply because `global` is an empty object `{}` while running at production.
* After digging a while, I deleted `swcMinify: true` from 'next.config.js' and recompiled the project
* The error disappears.
* Wild guess is that `swcMinify` might have some bug ~_~.

### Expected Behavior

The crash resulted from empty `global` caused by swcMinify disappears.

### To Reproduce

Write a sample project which uses `tui-calendar @ 1.15.3` under `nextjs @ 12.1.0`.

author: Meriegg

title: SSG should be supported in the Edge runtime
body:
We currently only support SSR in the Edge runtime.

Check [RFC: Switchable Next.js Runtime](#34179) for more details.
author: shuding

title: Support for Suspense-based data fetching in Fizz
body: Currently, Next.js still recommends `getStaticProps` and `getServerSideProps` for data fetching even with Fizz (React 18 streaming SSR and Server Components). In the longer term, we should support a built-in API in Next.js to do Suspense-based data fetching.
author: shuding

title: Nonce not applied to router scripts
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

   Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.3
      npm: 7.24.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Vivaldi latest

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

When locking down a site with nonce-based CSP, the nonce isn't applied to `next/link`s, which appear to prefetch by default via the router. The router appears to prefetch via the `prefetchViaDom` method.

### Expected Behavior

I thought it would pick it up from `next/head`s nonce, or even `NextScript`s nonce, but no. It should get the nonce from somewhere and utilize it router's script injections.

### To Reproduce

1. Generate a nonce and set the CSP http header in _document (csp has `script-src: nonce-rAnd0m`).
2. Also attach the nonce to `Head` and `NextScript` in _document.
3. Add the default getInitialProps code to _app to disable SSG.
3. Make a couple of pages that link to each other (using next/link).
4. When viewing in prod, you'll see CSP errors in the console. Also nonce-less `link` tags in the `head` and nonce-less `script` tags in the `body`.

title: Build times with swc are the same speed as babel
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Feb 19 06:37:35 UTC 2020
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.5.2
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

I was expecting the swc compiler to greatly improve my build times compared to babel. But after testing the build times on vercel, they seem to be almost identical.

In the repoduction repo below, the speed with swc deploys in 1m 47s. The babel version deploys in 1m 55s - which seems to in margin of error.

### Expected Behavior

Building with swc should be much faster.

### To Reproduce

I originally discovered this on one of my own repos, but I can't share it publicly. However, I was able to reproduce by forking the [Supabase website](https://github.com/frozberry/supabase).

1) Deploy the project on vercel without a `.babelrc`
2) Add a `.babelrc` file with the following: 

```
{
    "presets": ["next/babel"],
    "plugins": []
}
```
3) Deploy on vercel
4) Redeploy the babel version, making sure to not use the build cache.
5) Compare build times

author: armedi

title: NextApiRequest should have separate in-path parameters and query arguments.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

As it is now NextApiRequests have one property **query** and in this property there are mixed up both in-path parameters and query arguments. It would be super helpful I could somehow find out which belong where. This causes huge problems when I try to validate all my requests against OpenAPI yaml files. It is basically impossible to use both at the same time.

For example when FE makes axios call to `/api/user/12345/posts?limit=25`.
and my structure looks like this:
```
pages
-api
--user
---[id]
----posts.ts
```

then I get **query** object like this one `{ id: '12345', limit: '25' }`


### Describe the solution you'd like

I propose separate properties for both parameters and query arguments.

so when I have a NextApiRequest I would be able to simply deconstruct it:

`const { query, params } = request;`

and would get `{ limit: '25' } `and `{ id: '12345' }`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

property query would have objects instead of values that would describe parameters and query arguments.

```
{
  id: {
    value: '12345',
    type: 'param',
  },
  limit: {
    value: '25',
    type: 'query',
  },
}
```

body:
### Verify canary release

Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.6.1
      npm: 7.20.3
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.24.1
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Firefox/Chrome latest

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

When adding the dependency `@elastic/react-search-ui` our CSP is triggering on the use of Eval in the code. 
![afbeelding](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2519243/156781001-6090beee-918e-406e-bbfe-a513dbcb044a.png)

The only reference to eval seems to be coming from 2 prepackaged dependencies in Nextjs; assert.js & util.js:
![afbeelding](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2519243/156786278-ea2005ba-b426-485d-849f-4308aba64895.png)

It seems to be a polyfill in webpack but I'm not sure what is triggering it and why it is added to the clientside scripts.

### Expected Behavior

That Eval is not used anywhere in the bundled code so that our CSP doesn't block files that include which breaks things.

### To Reproduce

Repo with a nextjs starter and @elastic/react-search-ui: https://github.com/eelcobosklopper/next-eval-issue
When you build the code and search in the .next folder with `%eval%` you can see it in the code. 

![afbeelding](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2519243/156787443-6ac3c6b9-8940-4146-a7fc-fd059eff1f6f.png)

title: Attempting to use Streaming SSR (Alpha) produces TypeError: readable.getReader is not a function
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 15.6.0: Thu Jun 21 20:07:40 PDT 2018; root:xnu-3248.73.11~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.8.0
      npm: 7.21.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 18.0.0-rc.1-next-05c283c3c-20220302
      react-dom: 18.0.0-rc.1-next-05c283c3c-20220302

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

I updated to `next@latest` and `react@next` and attempted to enable Streaming SSR as per the instructions [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/react-18/streaming).

I tried using both `nodejs` and `edge` for the runtime option in `next.config.js`, but in both cases when attempting to run the app in development mode and load the page, I get the following error in the console, and the page fails to load:

```
error - unhandledRejection: TypeError: readable.getReader is not a function

TypeError: readable.getReader is not a function
    at pipeTo (/node_modules/next/dist/server/render.js:1262:29)
    at pipeThrough (/node_modules/next/dist/server/render.js:1283:5)
    at /node_modules/next/dist/server/render.js:1164:69
    at Array.reduce (<anonymous>)
    at /node_modules/next/dist/server/render.js:1164:39
    at runMicrotasks (<anonymous>)
    at processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:96:5)
```

Looks like the error is happening here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/server/render.tsx#L1899

Additionally, when using `nodejs` I additionally see this warning: `WARNING: Missing strong random number source`.

### Expected Behavior

Running the app in development mode with Streaming SSR enabled runs without errors and the page is able to load.

### To Reproduce

- Import this sandbox into a repo: https://codesandbox.io/s/fervent-star-xorduv
- `yarn && yarn start`
- Load `localhost:3000`

title: SWC Transpilation is incorrect
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

/bin/sh: pnpm: command not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Mon Aug 30 06:12:21 PDT 2021; root:xnu-7195.141.6~3/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.11.1
      npm: 8.0.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

n/a

### Describe the Bug

Looks like this is fixed in Next Canary, but I'll create the issue anyway, so hopefully someone can find it. If you do ... this is fixed in next 12.1.1-canary.6

---

The full repo is here: https://github.com/arackaf/swc-repro 

You can clone, npm i, and run the next app. Click the green button, with the dev tools open, and you should hit a debugger, with some comments above it, followed by an error.

Basically, the issue is (was) that `this` was transpiled wrongly in a non-arrow function. Relevant bit of code is below, full context in the linked repo above. 

```js
this.simplify = geoTransform({
  point() {
    // ERROR IS HERE. At runtime, the `this` value from `this.stream.point`, below, is transformed to `_this`,
    // with a value bound to the class component's `this` value, which has no stream object.

    // Note in the dev tools that `this` does indeed have a stream object on it, with a point method therein, which can be called.
    // But regardless, at a more basic level, the point method is NOT an arrow, and so references to `this` inside of it should NOT be transformed
    debugger;
    this.stream.point();
  },
```

### Expected Behavior

`this` should not be transpiled in a non-arrow function. 

### To Reproduce

See repo above

title: ISR not working consistently in 12.10.0?
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jan 12 22:22:42 PST 2022; root:xnu-7195.141.19~2/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Since upgrading to `12.1.0`, pages using ISR are not consistently being revalidated automatically. When this issue does occur on a page, it will not be refreshed with the new data until a new build is run.

I have confirmed that this is neither a browser cache issue or an issue with our CDN (Cloudflare). The stale data is seen by anyone on any browser, and I have purged our CDN cache completely (which only caches JS anyway).

### Expected Behavior

Pages using ISR should revalidate in the specified time period.

### To Reproduce

1. Create page that fetches data using `getStaticProps` with `revalidate: 300`
2. Update the data source
3. Wait 5 minutes
4. The stale data is still there
5. Wait 5 more minutes or even a full day
6. Still stale
7. Manually trigger a revalidate using `unstable_revalidate`
8. The data is fresh!

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

i am using the `postcss-custom-media` plugin, and when i run `next build` the first time (i.e. when the `.next` folder does not yet exist), everything works fine. however, after subsequent builds, the `@custom-media` at rules are not correctly replaced anymore.

### Expected Behavior

`@custom-media` rules should be properly replaced on every build.

### To Reproduce

1. `git clone git@github.com:stefanprobst/next-issue-postcss-custom-media`
2. `cd next-issue-postcss-custom-media/`
3. `yarn`
4. `yarn build && yarn start`
5. Open `http://localhost:3000/` and observe the background color changing when adjusting the viewport width.
6. Also, check `cat .next/static/css/XXX.css` to see the transformed media queries (e.g. `@media (min-width:30rem)`)
7. Edit `nano components/Hero.module.css` and chage a background color, e.g. change `background: lime` to `background: yellow`
8. Run  `yarn build && yarn start` again
9. Open `http://localhost:3000/` and observe that the background color does *not* change anymore when adjusting the viewport width
10. Also check `cat .next/static/css/XXX.css` to see that now the `@custom-media` rules have *not* been replaced (they still show e.g. `@media (--screen-sm)`)
author: stefanprobst

title: Default locale optimization for On-Demand Revalidate.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Following the official localization config, on build default locale files are generated in two places.
E.g. default locale `en`, is deployed on root directory `/` and `/en` directory.

When using the On Demand Revalidate Beta. `unstable_revalidate()` has to be called twice to replace both the file in root and `/en` directory.

I spot this problem when i first reloaded the page it initally gets the file from root or in directory, not sure. Then when i use react routes it gets the other file. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

1. Declare a standard how to revalidate the default locale paths either: `/en/contact` or `/contact`
2. Then revalidate automatically copy the generated file to the two destinations, root `/` and directory `/en`

### Describe alternatives you've considered

First i check if default locale is being revalidated.
Then i call the `unstable_revalidate()` twice with locale on it and the other without locale, e.g `/contact` and `/en/contact`.
That might cause the same data to be fetched twice, also the revalidate process 2x longer.

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Firefox Developer Edition 98.0b10 - Firefox 97.0.2 - Chromium 99.0.4844.51

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

In getServerSideProps and in getStaticProps, if I try to redirect to another locale by adding a locale prefix to the destination, the whole destination is added to the current locale prefix.
Ex.
```
return { redirect: { destination: '/fr/pathname' } }
```
Trying to visit the page containing the above redirection in its getServerSideProps or getStaticProps from the `/nl` page get me redirected to `/nl/fr/pathname` (which fail in the getStaticProps case).

### Expected Behavior

I expected to be correctly redirected to the new locale url, like `/fr/pathname`.

title: Dependency of a .cjs build may not be loaded correctly
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

$ /code/stateof/stateofjs-next/node_modules/.bin/next info
/bin/sh: 1: pnpm: not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #33~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Mon Feb 7 14:25:10 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.2
      npm: 6.14.15
      Yarn: 1.22.5
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

Done in 1.15s.


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

I am building a library of components.

- Package "my-package-tsup" has shared (ESM, IIFE, CJS), client-only (ESM, IIFE) and server-only (CJS, ESM) exports
- "my-package-tsup/server" (CJS) depends on "my-package-tsup-dependency" (CJS and ESM).
- So, server entrypoints are using CJS, while I allow shared code to use either CJS or ESM, with an `exports` field like this:
```js
{
  "name": "my-package-tsup",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "",
  "main": "./dist/shared/index.js",
  "type": "module",
  "files": [
    "dist/"
  ],
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "node": "./dist/server/index.cjs",
      "import": "./dist/shared/index.js"
    },
    "./server": "./dist/server/index.cjs", => this is the one that seems problematic.
    "./client": "./dist/client/index.js"
  },
```

So, when importing "my-package-tsup/server", Next will correctly load the "dist/server/index.cjs" file. But when this CJS file require it's subdependency, using a `require` call, it will try to pick the .js ESM export instead of sticking to the .cjs CJS export.


Here is the package.json of the dependency:
```js
{
  "name": "my-package-tsup-dependency",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "",
  "main": "./dist/shared/index.js",
  "type": "module",
  "files": [
    "dist/"
  ],
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "node": "./dist/server/index.cjs", => this is the one that should be preferred but instead Next.js complains about trying to require an ESM module. It might be tricked by the "type":"module" and not respect the exports for dependencies, while it works ok for top-level packages
      "import": "./dist/shared/index.js"
    },
    "./server": "./dist/server/index.cjs",
    "./client": "./dist/client/index.js"
  },
```

**To rephrase this:**
If a CJS top level package requires one if it's dependency, the `exports` field of the dependency does not seem to be respected. The ESM export will be loaded, instead of the CJS export.

### Expected Behavior

CommonJS dependencies should also use the CommonJS export of their own subdependencies.

### To Reproduce

It's not the easiest to reproduce:

- I can reproduce consistenly with this library of package: https://github.com/VulcanJS/vulcan-npm/tree/feature/datatable
```
git clone 
git checkout feature/datatable
yarn && yarn run build && yarn run publish:local
```
It will rely on Yalc, so to test you'll need to set it up in a Next app.
Then import `@vulcanjs/graphql/server` in your Next app and you'll end up with the dreaded `https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/import-esm-externals` error


- I am trying to build a simpler repro here: https://github.com/VulcanJS/npm-the-right-way/tree/feature/try-dep

Clone, install, and open the "with-tsup" page http://localhost:3000/with-tsup

I seem to be able to have this message: `Module not found: Package path . is not exported from package /code/npm-the-right-way/demo-next-app/node_modules/my-package-tsup-dependency (see exports field in /code/npm-the-right-way/demo-next-app/node_modules/my-package-tsup-dependency/package.json)`.

It's different from the error I have in the other package but could be related, as if "exports" were not understood correctly.

This issue might related: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34956

title: PostCSS configuration error with `tailwindcss/nesting` together with `postcss-nesting`
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```bash
  Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.3.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Using the `postcss-nesting` nesting plugin as described in the [Tailwind documentation](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/using-with-preprocessors#nesting) (see section about `postcss-nesting`) results in an error:

```Error: A PostCSS Plugin was passed as an array but did not provide its configuration ('tailwindcss/nesting').```

The PostCSS configuration used is the following:

```js
// postcss.config.js

module.exports = {
  plugins: {
    'postcss-import': {},
    'tailwindcss/nesting': 'postcss-nesting',
    tailwindcss: {},
    'postcss-preset-env': {
      features: { 'nesting-rules': false },
    },
  },
};
```

It looks like it does not resolve `postcss-nesting` to a valid config for `tailwindcss/nesting` or there is a check in place to force plugin values to be objects instead of a strings. I also tried the following without luck:

```js
// postcss.config.js

module.exports = {
  plugins: {
    'postcss-import': {},
    'tailwindcss/nesting': {'postcss-nesting' : {}},
    tailwindcss: {},
    'postcss-preset-env': {
      features: { 'nesting-rules': false },
    },
  },
};
```

the above results in a tailwind error: `Error: tailwindcss/nesting should be loaded with a nesting plugin.`

The configuration as provided in the tailwind docs has worked for me when using other non next.js setups, so I asume the issue is with next.js and the way it handles postcss.config.js

### Expected Behavior

For the configuration to work as expected.

### To Reproduce

Go to this sandbox: https://codesandbox.io/s/white-snow-um2w00 see the error in the terminal.

To test, remove the config file and the css import in `pages/_app.js` and all works fine.

title: next start: Port is opened even if the server cannot be started
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: 6.11.0
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.6
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

When starting a Next.js server using `next start`, it immediately opens the port. Even if the server crashes right after opening the port, for example due to an error inside `next.config.js`. It always first opens the port, does some initialization (which takes a few seconds based on the app size), then crashes.

This behavior causes some PaaS and Infrastructure-Setups to assume the server is ready and healthy, because they only check if the port was opened. This can cause unhealthy and crashing servers being deployed.

The above scenario with an error inside `next.config.js` happened to me inside a dockerized environment. Inside the `next.config.js`, a file was being required which was available inside the build layer, but not in the final image. This caused the image to build fine, but the server crashed a few seconds after starting the image. Because the next server opened the port immediately and then crashed, the PaaS (GCP CloudRun) did not catch the unhealthy container and it got deployed to production. The PaaS then continued to restart this container again and again, because the opened port indicated that the server started in a healthy state, which it didn't.
If the port would not have been opened, the unhealthy image would not have gotten deployed.

### Expected Behavior

The port should only be opened when the server started in a healthy and ready state.

### To Reproduce

1. Create a new app: `npx create-next-app@latest`
2. Add `require('./bad.js')` to the top of next.config.js
3. Create an empty file `./bad.js`
4. Run `npm run build`  -> build successful
5. Delete `./bad.js`
6. Run `npm start`

Output:

```
ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000
error - Failed to load next.config.js, see more info here https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/next-config-error
Error: Cannot find module './bad.js'
```

I confirmed that TCP Port 3000 was open for a short amount of time.

### Describe the Bug

I opened this issue yesterday [https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35113](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35113), but it seems I hadn't dug deep enough, and so I reproduced the wrong thing. We actually do have a server crash.

The crash happens under the following circumstances:
* There is a Next.js catch all page route, like `pages/[...catchAll].js`
* The Next.js server is started with dd-trace running, like this `NODE_OPTIONS='--require ./datadog' next start` and `datadog.js` has this content: `require('dd-trace').init()`. This applies to any version of dd-trace between 1.2.0 and the latest 2.3.1.
* There is a request to an api route with an invalid URI `/api/%ff`.

It produces the following stack trace from Next.js

```
./node_modules/next/dist/shared/lib/router/utils/route-matcher.js:18
                throw new _utils.DecodeError('failed to decode param');
                      ^
DecodeError: failed to decode param
    at decode (./node_modules/next/dist/shared/lib/router/utils/route-matcher.js:18:23)
    at ./node_modules/next/dist/shared/lib/router/utils/route-matcher.js:26:80
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at ./node_modules/next/dist/shared/lib/router/utils/route-matcher.js:26:67
    at Array.forEach (<anonymous>)
    at Object.match (./node_modules/next/dist/shared/lib/router/utils/route-matcher.js:22:29)
    at NextNodeServer.handleApiRequest (./node_modules/next/dist/server/base-server.js:538:39)
    at processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:96:5)
error Command failed with exit code 1.
```

I don't know whether the actual error is in next.js or in dd-trace, but they are both black boxes to me and I only get a stack trace containing next.js code. I have cross-posted this in the dd-trace repo: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-js/issues/1898


### Expected Behavior

The server responds with 404 or 400.

### To Reproduce

* Clone this repo: https://github.com/jakst/nextjs-router-crash-reproduction
* Run `yarn build`
* Run `yarn start`
* Visit http://localhost:3000/api/%ff

title: `module.exports` is no longer supported with `import`
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
    Arch: arm64
    Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
Binaries:
  Node: 16.14.0
  npm: 8.5.3
  Yarn: 1.22.17
  pnpm: 6.30.1
Relevant packages:
  next: 12.1.1-canary.7
  react: 18.0.0-rc.1
  react-dom: 18.0.0-rc.1
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 98.0.4758.109

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

So far, the Next.js `canary` channel supported importing files that used `module.exports` with `import`.

After I upgraded from `12.1.1-canary.3` to `12.1.1-canary.7`, however, this syntax stopped being supported and I'm now getting the following error:

```
warn  - ./utils/tailwind.ts?__sc_server__
Attempted import error: '../tailwind.config?__sc_server__' does not contain a default export (imported as 'tailwindConfig').
error - ReferenceError: exports is not defined
```

The file contains the following line at the top:

```javascript
import tailwindConfig from '../tailwind.config';
```

Changing it to this made it work:

```javascript
const tailwindConfig = require('../tailwind.config');
```

For extra context, the `tailwind.config.js` file contains the following syntax:

```javascript
module.exports = {
...
```

Note that I'm using React Server Components on the Edge Runtime.

### Expected Behavior

Perhaps it is expected that the old behavior no longer works, but I don't think so.

I'd expect the old behavior (using `import` to load `module.exports`) to still work.

### To Reproduce

Import a `.js` file that uses `module.exports` from a file that uses `import`.

  next: 12.1.1-canary.7
  react: 18.0.0-rc.1
  react-dom: 18.0.0-rc.1
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 98.0.4758.109

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

With version `12.1.1-canary.7` of `next`, a `Link` pointing to a route on which you've initially entered the application longer does anything when clicked.

It's possible to reach any route via `Link`, just not the page on which you've loaded the app initially.

The URL in the address bar of the browser is set to the correct URL, but no page transition occurs. I also checked the console, but there aren't any errors. Same for the terminal.

Note that I'm using React Server Components on the Edge Runtime.

### Expected Behavior

I'd expect a `Link` that points to a page on which I've loaded the app initially to still work.

### To Reproduce

1. On any page of your app, add a `Link` with `href` set to any other route
1. Stop your devserver
2. Clean your cache with `rm -rf .next` (just to be sure)
3. Start your devserver
4. Load the app on the URL that you've defined as `href` for the `Link`
5. Navigate to the route that contains the `Link`
6. Click the `Link`
7. Notice that the URL in the address bar changes to the value of `href`, but no page transition occurs.

title: Translation of react-intl when using values
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.15.0
      npm: 6.14.8
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.7
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### Which example does this report relate to?

with-react-intl

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When using values in combination with react-intl/formatjs for example:

```js
<FormattedMessage defaultMessage="Time on site: {seconds}" values={{ seconds: 0 }}/>
// or
intl.formatMessage({ defaultMessage: "Time on site: {seconds}" }, { seconds: 0 });
```

The string gets always translated in the defaultMessage, even though translations exists.
Even when looking in the context of `react-intl` the message is correctly provided and even translated!

```js
const intl = useIntl()
console.log(intl.messages.TbqYJ9) // "Zeit auf dieser Seite: {seconds}"
console.log(intl.formatMessage({ id "TbqYJ9"}, { seconds: 0}); // Zeit auf dieser Seite: 0
```

Then i turned the javascript off - the Static Page gets translated correctly. So that means that the react-intl provider has somthing to do with it i reckon:

```js
export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) {
  const { locale, defaultLocale } = useRouter()
  return (
    <IntlProvider
      locale={locale}
      defaultLocale={defaultLocale}
      messages={pageProps.intlMessages}
    >
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </IntlProvider>
  )
}
```

The funny thing is that it works when working with `yarn dev` - as soon as you build the project, the javascript translation does not work anymore.

### Expected Behavior

When switching to another language the strings should be translated in the different language

### To Reproduce

I created a repository to demonstrate the issue: https://github.com/ChiefORZ/react-intl-nextjs-production-bug

```sh
git clone https://github.com/ChiefORZ/react-intl-nextjs-production-bug
cd react-intl-nextjs-production-bug
yarn
yarn build
yarn start
```

Then open your browser at localhost:3000 and switch to German or French - the string *Time on site: {seconds}* will not be translated, but the rest of the site will be respectively

### Describe the Bug

As of Next12 it looks like NextJS is adding a data attribute (`data-nscript`) to scripts rendered by the built in Script Tag. This behavior breaks a key third script in our application which uses data attributes for configuring and therefore prevents upgrading from Next11 to Next12. As far as I can tell this behavior is undocumented and not possible to control. Please see the attached screen shots for the impact of this data attribute for our use case:

![NextScript1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20232215/157266381-1cae753a-08a2-46ee-969e-f14ac1b7132f.png)

### Expected Behavior

I'm not entirely sure why NextJS is adding this data attribute, but it would be helpful if we could somehow opt out of it.

### To Reproduce

Use the `Script` tag in Next12 or later.
Note the `data-nscript` attribute that is added to the DOM.

title: next/head <meta> tags for pages overriding default _app.js <meta> tags  are rendered in the browser but not visible to facebook debugger.
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

sorry this is not working. 

i am running 

nextjs 12
node 16.12.1
yarn 1.22.5

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

chrome

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

VPS

### Describe the Bug

Summary: I am trying to create fb and twitter metadata for my whole site by defining a <Head> with metadata in _app.js with overrides for specific components using the identical set of <meta> tags with some local overrides.

Implementation: I have a two page site with _app.js, one.js and two.js files. I have added  a <Head> section into _app.js file and a <Head> section in one.js with some overrides. I have used the keys to ensure that duplicates are not made and when i load the pages in the browser I can see that this works correctly and component specific overrides are presented. 

The relevant metadata is as follows and is contained in both _app.js and one.js (but not two.js which I expect to use _app.js metadata). Use of variables enables me to use an identical metadata and set component specific overrides.

``` 
<Head> 
      ...   other stuff here
     <meta property="og:title" content={title} key="og-title"/>
      <meta property="og:description" content={description} key="og-desc"/>
      <meta property="og:url" content={router.pathname} key="og-url"/>
      <meta property="og:image" content={image} key="og-image" />
      <meta property="og:site_name" content="mysitename" key="og-site" />
      <meta name="twitter:title" content={title} key="tw-title"/>
      <meta name="twitter:description" content={description} key="tw-desc"/>
      <meta name="twitter:image" content={image} key="tw-image"/>
      <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image" key="tw-card"/>
</Head>
```

Result:

Summary: things work as expected in the browser but facebook debugger is not picking up the component specific changes.

In the browser I can see that the correct metadata is loaded for one.js (presenting the component specific overrides) and two.js (presenting the metadata from _app.js). However the facebook debugger is only picking up metadata from _app.js not from one.js - so all pages have the same metadata. 


### Expected Behavior

Both the facebook debugger and the client browser to load the same metadata. Facebook debugger is somehow not collecting the page specific metadata overrides.

### To Reproduce

I have a live site running here:

https://next18.syntapse.co.uk/two doesnt define its own metadata so loads default <meta> tags from _app.js using keys to avoid duplication

https//next18.syntapse.co.uk/one loads overrided <meta> tags using keys to avoid duplication

This works as expected in the browser. Load this into a browser and note the differences in metadata. Note the metadata changes from the default to the override. 

Load this into the facebook debugger and note that /one loads the same metadata as /two and also / url.

title: PerformanceObserver.observe() breaks inside next/image on chrome 69
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
  Platform: darwin
  Arch: arm64
  Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.1.0: Wed Oct 13 17:33:24 PDT 2021; root:xnu-8019.41.5~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
Binaries:
  Node: 15.14.0
  npm: 7.7.6
  Yarn: 3.0.1
  pnpm: N/A
Relevant packages:
  next: 12.0.10
  react: 0.0.4
  react-dom: 0.0.4
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 69.0.3497.106

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

TL,DR
`performanceObserver.observe()` throws an exception when not provided with the `entryTypes` option **in older browsers like Chrome 69**.

Description:
When using next/image, it internally attaches performance observer for LargestContentfulPaint events.
On older browsers like Chromium 69 this fails with the following error:
`TypeError: Failed to execute 'observe' on 'PerformanceObserver': required member entryTypes is undefined.`

While the current implementation follows the latest w3c specs which specifies that `performanceObserver.observe()` must be passed either `entryTypes` array or `type` and `buffered` as options, next/image usses the later here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/image.tsx#L519

But Chrome 69 enforces that `entryTypes` must be provided to `observe()`, hence it throws an exception.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9393546/157623138-3ced7113-c562-42fe-ba76-0816c397a085.png)


### Expected Behavior

`performanceObserver.observe()` should be wrapped inside a try/catch block

### To Reproduce

Use next/image in an older browser like Chrome 69

body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #33~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Mon Feb 7 14:25:10 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.30.0
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.9
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

currently, the css minifier (i think this is the compiled-in cssnano-simple) produces invalid css from the following:

```css
h1 {
  --border-width: 8px 2px;

  border-width: var(--border-width);
  border-style: solid;
  border-color: hotpink;
}
```

results in:

```css
h1{--border-width:8px 2px;border:var(--border-width) solid hotpink}
```

which is invalid.

note that this is currently also handled incorrectly in upstream `cssnano` with the default preset, but works correctly with the "lite" preset. cf. https://github.com/cssnano/cssnano/issues/1354

### Expected Behavior

don't collapse border properties into shorthand when custom properties are encountered

### To Reproduce

- checkout https://github.com/stefanprobst/next-issue-css-shorthand and compare the results of `yarn dev` vs `yarn build && yarn start`

title: `next lint` command doesn't support overrides
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP PREEMPT Wed Dec 29 18:08:07 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 17.3.0
      npm: 8.3.0
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.9
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

If you have an eslint config that uses the `overrides` key to define different settings for different files, the `next lint` doesn't notice it.

For example, if you have the following eslint config in your `package.json`:
```json
  "eslintConfig": {
    "overrides": [
      {
        "files": [
          "**/*.tsx"
        ],
        "extends": [
          "next/core-web-vitals"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
```

You will receive the next warn:
```
warn  - The Next.js plugin was not detected in your ESLint configuration. See https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/eslint#migrating-existing-config
```


### Expected Behavior

Handles the `overrides` config or simply **warns about this limitation**.

**Why is this important?**
A lot of people are getting this warning and probably haven't noticed. Just search for `The Next.js plugin was not detected in your ESLint configuration` around the web (Google, Next.js issues, etc) for evidence of this.
The worst-case scenario of this is that there are probably many users who are creating applications believing that they are complying with Core Web Vital directives but they are not.

**Is this easy to fix?**
I think not, ​so I recommend documenting this in a better way.

I tried to read an `override` configuration and this is the hacky code I ended up with to make the `next lint` command work only for my single-use case (see my case in **To Reproduce** section):
```diff
# https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fbb8536956beb766aa401d83acd2c8d358e2dd11/packages/next/lib/eslint/runLintCheck.ts:143
-      const completeConfig: Config = await eslint.calculateConfigForFile(
-        configFile
-      )
+      let completeConfig: Config
+      if (eslintOptions?.extensions) {
+        // ESLint doesn't expose its entire API publicly.
+        const { getESLintPrivateMembers } = await import(path.join(require.resolve('eslint'), '..', 'eslint', 'eslint'))
+        const { getCLIEngineInternalSlots } = await import(path.join(require.resolve('eslint'), '..', 'cli-engine', 'cli-engine'))
+        const { cliEngine } = getESLintPrivateMembers(eslint)
+        const { configArrayFactory } = getCLIEngineInternalSlots(cliEngine)
+        completeConfig = await configArrayFactory.getConfigArrayForFile(configFile).extractConfig(`${process.cwd()}/*${eslintOptions.extensions}`).toCompatibleObjectAsConfigFileContent()
+      } else {
+        completeConfig = await eslint.calculateConfigForFile(configFile)
+      }
```


### To Reproduce

1 - Config `package.json` with:
```json
  "eslintConfig": {
    "overrides": [
      {
        "files": [
          "**/*.tsx"
        ],
        "extends": [
          "next/core-web-vitals"
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
```

2 - Run `next lint .  --ext tsx`

author: champseo2017

title: Static video imports
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Like how it's current possibly to [statically import `.png`, `.jpg` and `.webp` images](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/image-optimization#local-images):

```js
import profilePic from '../public/me.png'
// => gets a { src: string, height: number, width: number, blurDataURL?: string }
```

it'd be nice if we could also import videos to get a similar object providing at least a URL to the video.ge

### Describe the solution you'd like

e.g. being able to do

```js
import animation from '../public/animation.webm'
// => at least { src: string }, possibly also including with and height
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

A full `<Video>` component like the `<Image>` component could be useful as well, but would require a lot more processing, is presumably harder to implement, and doesn't currently support statically exported sites. (There's also https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/20323 and https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/26388 for that.)

We _can_ currently place the video files in `/public/` and manually write the link to it, but then we don't get warnings at build time when those links break, and doesn't allow us to colocate a video and the component it's used in.

title: Referrer Header Validation for prevent vulnerability CSRF
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

594 / 5.000
Resultados de traducción
Hello how are you.
I am investigating how to validate the referrer header of a resource (image, css, js, etc) in nextjs and prevent them from being loaded when the URL is not allowed, in short, to validate the URLs through an allowed list of URLs.
I didn't find any info yet other than using a custom nextjs server, but according to their documentation this has static resource optimization issues.
I mention this because in a pentest report of my work, they indicated a CSRF vulnerability because the application in nextjs does not validate the header referer of the resources. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like to be able to set in the next.config.js an allowed list of urls for the referrer header

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently I read that I can validate the resource referer header using a custom next.js server, however this brings performance issues according to its own documentation.
