title: For loop does not have block scope in client side generated code
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

▶ npx next info
/bin/sh: 1: yarn: not found
/bin/sh: 1: pnpm: not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Aug 25 23:20:18 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.1
      npm: 6.14.13
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.10
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

The behavior of a function that relies on block-level scope in for-of loop is different between server run and client run.

Take the following code snippet:

```ts
function logValues() {
  for (const i of [1,2,3,4]) {
    Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(i));
  }
}
```

When run from `getServerSideProps` it logs

```
1
2
3
4
```

as expected.

The same function when run on the browser logs:

```
4
4
4
4
```

I suspect this is because of how it is transpiled to es5. I have verified that using just TSC or babel+webpack (with bare minimum configuration) to transpile does not have this issue. So there must be some configuration in NextJS that's causing this issue.

### Expected Behavior

The transpiled/bundled code should behave exactly as the original code. In my case this bug meant that a function used to fetch data had completely different behavior when fetching data server-side vs client-side.

### To Reproduce

I have a reproduction of the bug in a scenario very similar to how I discovered it in a repo. When you run the app, you'll see three data values that was fetched server-side, but when you hit "Fetch client side" button it runs the same function client-side and we only have one data value.

Repo: https://github.com/squgeim/nextjs_for_loop_bug/blob/main/pages/index.tsx
Deployment: https://nextjs-for-loop-bug.vercel.app/

At its core, the bug can be replicated by putting the following snippet in a function and call the function on the server and client:

```ts
for (const i of [1,2,3,4]) {
  Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(i));
}
```

i18n: docs example is deprecated:
at _middleware: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing#prefixing-the-default-locale 

Now with 12.1, nextjs suggest to use clone() of nextUrl and replace relative URLs by absolute.

"URLs is malformed. Please use only absolute URLs" 
https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-relative-urls

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

Can't use Nextjs example code at docs with url relatives: 
```
const res = redirect(`/${ localeOrGeoCookie }${ nextUrl.pathname }`) // without clone it
```

I think to use clone will help:

```
const url = request.nextUrl.clone()
const res = NextResponse.redirect( `${url.origin}/${localeOrGeoCookie}` )
res.cookie('GEO_COUNTRY', country) // optional, I need to store geo country name
return res
```

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/i18n-routing#prefixing-the-default-locale
author: santyas

title: Support optional chaining by default
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I have had many **can't access property of undefined** errors and I could figure out why, although I have been using optional chaining.  After some search I found the reason and also the solution. I believe many people are affected by this, and are probably losing their minds like I was, because they can't figure out why this is happening :(

I know this is already a known bug but I would like to propose adding Babel's solution for this _by default_, as explained here: [@babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining](https://babeljs.io/docs/en/babel-plugin-proposal-optional-chaining)

I don't know if you already are aware of this, but didn't do it because it may affect something else. However I noticed this and thought it would be beneficial to be added. If I'm wrong just ignore this 👍 


### Describe the solution you'd like

It's just a matter of installing the package provided by Babel, like so: 

```
npm install --save-dev @babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining
```

and then adding this code in a **.babelrc** config file
```
{
  "plugins": ["@babel/plugin-proposal-optional-chaining"]
}
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

That would already be all :D

      next: 12.1.1-canary.10
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When navigating within the same `page` (E.g. the previous page and the current page are both `/pages/[id].js`), the React tree won't get a full update, causing the `next/image` component's `intersected` state not getting reset, causing the lazyload to fail.

### Expected Behavior

The image not in view should never be loaded.

### To Reproduce

I have provided a minimum reproduction on Stackblitz: https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-evf8rw

0. Open the Browser's Dev Tools, switch to `Network` Tab
1. Click link `Image1`
2. The image is not loaded (instead only a data URI gif is loaded), because the image is not in the viewport
3. Scroll down, the image is now loaded
4. Click link `Image2`
5. The new image is loaded even it is not in the viewport.

title: Slow start when lots of files on public folder
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #118-Ubuntu SMP Wed Mar 2 19:02:41 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.1
      npm: 6.14.15
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.10
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Increasing the number of files in the `/public` folder makes the next.js application startup to slow down while driving CPU and memory consumption up until the startup procedure ends or it crashes due to getting OOM. These are the numbers I'm observing in my dev box:
| # files in /public folder | startup time |
| ------------------------------|-----------------|
|    1K                             |  269.062ms |
|   200K                          |  1.550s        |
|    1M                            |   7.523s       |
|   10M                         |   1m27s263ms (**until OOM crash**) | 

By running a CPU profile on the process, it looks like most of the time is spent on the [recursiveReadDirSync](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/9f54b56bdea140f6aa474ebf39ed2cd98b3f7c8c/packages/next/server/lib/recursive-readdir-sync.t) function when called from [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/server/next-server.ts#L429) which seems to make sense with the observation.

### Expected Behavior

First of all. Is this to be expected? By looking at the documentation on the `public` folder from https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving, it doesn't mention any issues with serving many static files though it makes a recommendation on using a 3rd party service for persistent file storage. Is this the actual reason?

### To Reproduce

- Create the basic acc with `npx create-next-app`
- Create/Add the desired number of files in the `/public` folder.
- Run: `npm run build`
- Run `npm run start`  and check the CPU and memory usage and the time it takes to the server to be responsive depending on the number of files.

author: lydiahallie

title: Support _app directory to act as _app.js
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Organizing files of `pages` inside directories helps. The problem is that `_app` can't be a directory. So you end up with this situation

```
.
└── pages/
    ├── somePage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent1.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent2.js
    │   └── etc.js
    ├── anotherPage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   └── ...etc
    ├── _app.js
    └── _appComponent(where do I live? 😔).js
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Ideally, it should be possible to make `_app` a directory so that supporting components can live within it as well, like this

```
.
└── pages/
    ├── somePage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent1.js
    │   ├── somePageComponent2.js
    │   └── etc.js
    ├── anotherPage/
    │   ├── index.js
    │   └── ...etc
    └── _app/
        ├── index.js
        └── _appComponent(This is my home 😊).js
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Have an `_app` directory that `_app.js` imports from.
author: msafi

title: Append local network address (stdout)
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

If I were to spin up a new dev server with the command
```
npm run dev
```
I expect to be informed about the **network address where I can preview the app development on other devices**.

This would be an example of the output (taken from the [create-react-app](https://github.com/facebook/create-react-app) example. Something similar exists for Gatsby as well)
```
Local:            http://localhost:3000
On Your Network:  http://192.168.0.18:3000
```

I am referring to this part -> `On Your Network:  http://192.168.0.18:3000`.
This type of information is missing in the terminal output even though I can preview my Next.js app on other devices if I were to visit the "On Your Network" address on other devices.

Currently, when I start a local development server for the Next.js app, the only information I see in the terminal is
```
> next dev

ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000
```

but it would be nice if the output in the terminal was something like this
```
> next dev

ready - started server on 0.0.0.0:3000, url: http://localhost:3000, network: http://192.168.0.18:3000
```

As said, the "network" URL is already available and operational for the Next.js app development and I can preview the app on other devices, but the network URL information is not present in the terminal. My suggestion is to add that missing information when the local development server is started.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add the missing "network" URL information in the terminal output when the local development server is started.

Example
```
Local:            http://localhost:3000
On Your Network:  http://192.168.0.18:3000
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

/

title: Preflight cache for middleware should behave the same in dev and prod
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Enterprise
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.15

      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

In developement I added a middleware to "gate" a set of pages. While in development the middleware is call on every route change and the functionnality is working well.

Once deployed in production on Vercel the functionnality is not working anymore because the middleware is not called anymore on every route change.

I believe it's because of the preflight cache (view https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/32767).

It's confusing that the middleware doesn't behave the same in development and in production. It's very error prone.

To be honest I am not fully understanding why the preflight cache exist so my proposition might be silly. From a developer standpoint this functionnality doesn't makes me feels confident about my code :)

### Expected Behavior

The middleware preflich cache behave the same in development and in production.

### To Reproduce

- Have a middleware at the root of /pages with a console log everytime it's called
- Start the app with a production build
- Navigate between the pages

The console will not log on subsequent route changes.

title: Better monitoring / Improvements in nextjs server stdout (logs) output
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Motivation:
Better nextjs logging (especially for unhandled exceptions) to understand exactly context of possible errors/warnings and be able to respond very fast to those situations.

Hi, right now after `next build` when we start next server with `next start` we see output logs only if error happens.

I would like to be able to have more control here over
1. log format. Example print json output instead of line by line example that i copy below
```
Error: {"size":0,"timeout":0}
at getStaticProps (/usr/src/app/.next/server/pages/post/[id].js:3576:11)
at runMicrotasks (<anonymous>)
```
2. Ability to add meta/context to nextjs logs after specifying a json format. Example enrich all logs with request url as well, or with custom tracing field whatever developer wants.
In that case above log would look like {"message": "some message here", "url": "domain.com/someurl", "appVersion":"something", ...othermeta}
3. In above real log that I posted i see js file name, but my code is in typescript. How can we apply source maps so see ts file line  in log output in those unhandled errors ?

PS: This is not about wrapping everything in a try catch and logging to custom logger. We are doing that sending to newrelic, but many things happen and are not handled. In those cases next server prints the error but without formatting context and is not very useful, therefore my suggestion to having a great way like in other frameworks for full visibility



### Describe the solution you'd like

As described in feature request I want to be able to do those actions.
I think one way maybe can be configurations in next.config.js but as I don't know nextjs internals well I am open to any solution that is best fit

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Unfortunately nothing because is impossible currently to add meta and format next output to json.

title: Next won't build in Nx Monorepo
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```sh
❯ pnpm next info

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Wed Aug 25 23:20:18 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: 6.32.2
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.10
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

pnpm nx build

### Describe the Bug

When using an async function in middleware, for example:

```ts
async function echo(message: string): Promise<string> {
  return message;
}

export { echo };
```

Monorepo won't build failing with the following message:

```sh
❯ pnpm nx build

   ✔    1/1 dependent project tasks succeeded [0 read from cache]

   Hint: you can run the command with --verbose to see the full dependent project outputs

 ——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————


> nx run eph:build:production

info  - Checking validity of types
warn  - using beta Middleware (not covered by semver) - https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/beta-middleware
info  - Creating an optimized production build
Failed to compile.

../../node_modules/.pnpm/next@12.1.1-canary.10_fbf64ceed39fcaf258f74679fe7c79ca/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/regenerator-runtime/runtime.js
Dynamic Code Evaluation (e. g. 'eval', 'new Function') not allowed in Middleware pages/_middleware

Import trace for requested module:
../../libs/utility/src/lib/utility.ts
../../libs/utility/src/index.ts
./pages/_middleware.ts

Build failed because of webpack errors

 ——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

 >  NX   Ran target build for project eph and 1 task(s) it depends on (10s)

    ✖    1/2 failed
    ✔    1/2 succeeded [0 read from cache]
```

If async keyword is removed and refactored to synchronous code, it will build as expected.

### Expected Behavior

This project should build, as we are using vanilla functions (Edge runtime compatibility)

### To Reproduce

Clone the repo:

```sh
git clone https://github.com/jchamale/next-build-issue
cd next-build-issue
pnpm i 
pnpm nx build
```

Output:

```sh
❯ pnpm nx build

   ✔    1/1 dependent project tasks succeeded [0 read from cache]

   Hint: you can run the command with --verbose to see the full dependent project outputs

 ——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————


> nx run eph:build:production

info  - Checking validity of types
warn  - using beta Middleware (not covered by semver) - https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/beta-middleware
info  - Creating an optimized production build
Failed to compile.

../../node_modules/.pnpm/next@12.1.1-canary.10_fbf64ceed39fcaf258f74679fe7c79ca/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/regenerator-runtime/runtime.js
Dynamic Code Evaluation (e. g. 'eval', 'new Function') not allowed in Middleware pages/_middleware

Import trace for requested module:
../../libs/utility/src/lib/utility.ts
../../libs/utility/src/index.ts
./pages/_middleware.ts

Build failed because of webpack errors

 ——————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

 >  NX   Ran target build for project eph and 1 task(s) it depends on (10s)

    ✖    1/2 failed
    ✔    1/2 succeeded [0 read from cache]
```

To replicate from scratch we can initialize a monorepo then add a Next JS app:

```sh
pnpm nx g @nrwl/next:app eph
```

Then add a new library

```sh
 pnpm nx generate @nrwl/js:library --name=utility --buildable --compiler=swc
```

Then add the following function in utility library: 

```ts
async function echo(message: string): Promise<string> {
  return message;
}

export { echo };
```

And then using it inside the middleware.

title: Example+Doc page for "Where to store persistent/global data?"
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

With the advent of react-hooks and all the different ways to store sessions across cookies, jwt, state management, etc, I think there should be a page in the documentation of some best practices for this, or some strong recommendations.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Something that recommends how to store global information across the app for a user.  Outside of Authentication information, the other variables that go with the user.

Maybe a page that makes it easier to find the `with-iron-session` and how it ought to be used for session globals.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

For a while I was trying to keep some session information in the url and having conditional routing based on url information in the path with wild cards like `[id]` in the path and read it with `useRouter`, but that causes a lot of these kinds of errors during the prerendering page `npm run build`  steps.
```
Error: No router instance found.
You should only use "next/router" on the client side of your app.
```
This closed/expired issue page still looks definitive because it is a top hit in google when searching for how to do it in nextjs.
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/2252

I nearly jumped on `nookies` or `react-cookies`... then I found the example for `with-iron-session`.  I'm giving that a try since it matches most of my criteria.

react-hook-form recommends `Redux` or `little-state-machine` here  
https://react-hook-form.com/advanced-usage#WizardFormFunnel

body:
### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information Operating System: Platform: darwin Arch: x64 Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jun 23 00:26:31 PDT 2021; root:xnu-7195.141.2~5/RELEASE_X86_64 Binaries: Node: 12.22.10 npm: 6.14.16 Yarn: 1.22.17 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.0 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) _No response_ ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) _No response_ ### Describe the Bug For example, we have a `pages/[site].js` file: ```js import React from 'react' export const getStaticPaths = async () => { return { paths: [], fallback: 'blocking', } } export const getStaticProps = async (req) => { console.log('in getStaticProps') const v = Math.random() if (v < 0.7) { return { notFound: true } } return { props: { v }, revalidate: 10, } } const BlogIndexPage = ({v}) => { return
hello world {v}
} export default BlogIndexPage ``` in nextjs version 12.1.0, if `getStaticProps` return `{ notFound: true }` once, the `getStaticProps` function will never be called again even if the revalidate time expired! Version 11.1.0 works! ### Expected Behavior `getStaticProps` should be called again if it's `{ notFound: true }` ### To Reproduce Code above, using nextjs 12.1.0 Version 11.1.0 works.

author: naptestdev

title: Allow next/jest to transform selected node_modules
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

The next/jest config prevents any node_modules from being added to the transform list https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/acbd54322f447caaee09b1262e4687434ea0268c/packages/next/build/jest/jest.ts#L118

Some node_modules really do need transforming, e.g. https://github.com/sindresorhus/serialize-error/blob/main/index.js . This is a common enough pattern that it is explicitly mentioned in the Jest config docs https://jestjs.io/docs/configuration#transformignorepatterns-arraystring

Please allow the custom Jest config to override the next/jest assumptions

### Describe the solution you'd like

don't ignore custom transformIgnorePatterns like

```js
const customJestConfig = {
  transformIgnorePatterns: ["/node_modules/(?!(serialize-error)/)"],
};
```
it's a slightly tricky one, it might require a regex to detect node_modules matching patterns, or a separate config option in the `nextJest({...options})` invocation, e.g. `{transformNodeModules:["serialize-error"],}`.



### Describe alternatives you've considered

Setting up Jest manually

title: Middleware builds are using the package.json browser field
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

(not relevant)

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jan 12 22:22:42 PST 2022; root:xnu-7195.141.19~2/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

N/A

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

N/A

### Describe the Bug

Middleware webpack builds are using the "browser" field from package.json, which is surprising because middleware only run s in a server context.

With the following in your `package.json`:
```
  "browser": {
    "some-package": false,
  }
```  
`some-package` will not be defined in middleware handlers.
  

### Expected Behavior

The "browser" field from package.json is not used when building middleware code paths.

### To Reproduce

1. Add a package entry within the browser field in the project package.json, setting it's value to false
2. Import the package into middleware
3. Observe the package is undefined in the middleware runtime

body:

```
 next info

```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug



```Fast Refresh will perform a full reload when you edit a file that is imported by modules outside of the React rendering tree. It is also possible the parent component of the component you edited is a class component, which disables Fast Refresh. Fast Refresh requires at least one parent function component in your React tree.```

In tsconfig.json I have
```
"paths": {
      "@/core/*": ["src/core/*"],
    }
```

Nextjs mention that supports such optio here
https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/module-path-aliases
compiling all works great, issue is in dev mode that fast refresh doesn't work when I import in some `pages/test.tsx`
I do `import @/core/supercomponent.tsx` instead of `import '../core/supercomponent.tsx'`
second one works with HMR, first one using the ts alias breaks 

### Expected Behavior

It works without full reload when using typescript aliases

### To Reproduce

pages/somepage.tsx

```
import supercomp  from '@/core/supercomp';
```

tsconfig.json
```
...
    "paths": {
      "@/core/*": ["src/core/*"],
    }
```

![Screenshot 2022-03-16 at 18 03 43](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15630854/158646706-18415e66-07fe-456d-a951-5f5bb668bd78.png)
author: kristijorgji

title: SSG - Fallback Mode to CSR Dynamic Routes Not Generated During Build
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

When serving a Next app from the output of `next export` (using Next.js in SSG mode), I would like to be able to use `next/router` to route to dynamic routes that were not generated during the static build and to CSR those pages instead.

As a companion feature, the `/404` route will detect when the `asPath` matches the dynamic route and redirect the user there. Assuming the site operator has correctly configured fallback routing, this redirect would ensure users are automatically directed to the correct page with no additional Next.js configuration.

#### Use case

For a statically hosted Next.js app, use dynamic routing to display pages from a data set that changes too frequently to be integrated with the build process.

### Describe the solution you'd like

First, a new fallback mode should be introduced that enables the desired feature. When in the new fallback mode two potential strategies may implement the feature.

When routing to the dynamic path client side, either:
1. `getStaticProps` is run, and Its output is used to render the page client side.
2. A new page file static function export, let's call it `getPropsClientSide`, is run. This new function's output is merged into the `pageProps` returned by `getStaticProps` during the build process. The merged object is used to render the page client side.


### Describe alternatives you've considered

There is no way I am aware of that this can be achieved using out-of-the-box dynamic routing/path parameters in Next.js. If there is, I apologize for this feature request, but would ask that the documentation be updated to explain the strategy.

The only alternative is to forego path parameters and use query string parameters, or "clever" solutions like implementing CDN redirect rules and mock path parameters using the `as` argument of `router.push`;

### Describe the Bug

Setting custom `tsconfig.tsconfigPath` in `12.1.0` breaks path alias.

### Expected Behavior

Path alias should still work when using custom tsconfig file.

### To Reproduce

If you are using `compilerOptions.path` and set some custom paths then it only works if you are using NextJS default tsconfig.json.

If this exact tsconfig file is renamed and set via `typescript.tsconfigPath`, path alias are not picked up in node_module packages and build/dev breaks.

author: faradaytrs

title: Ability to override webpack devtool in development mode for detailed sourcemaps
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Next allows a custom webpack devtool for production but in development, it reverts any custom devtool to `eval-source-map`. It would be great if there is a way to override the default devtool in development mode to allow for more detailed modes such as `source-map`.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Overrides to `webpack.devtool` will be ignored in development mode as per the [documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/improper-devtool). I think it would be great if this override by next was removed.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

With Next 12, using `zeit/next-source-maps` became deprecated and overriding devtool in `next.config.js` doesn't seem to work. And I couldn't find other alternatives solution after a fairly exhaustive search of the web and the documentation.
author: suryarajendhran

title: Consider using RSLint instead of ESLint
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi,

So basically it's nothing new, Next.js already includes built-in linting, which works great, but considering recent changes from JS based tooling to Rust based tooling, I think a huge performance improvement can be made here too.

### Describe the solution you'd like

My proposal is to replace ESLint with RSLint, which is a linter written in Rust and it is 8x faster on a rather small 11k LOC project (0.05 sec vs 0.4 sec), and an incredible 113x times faster on a medium 42k LOC(0.4 sec vs 47 seconds)!.
I can only assume the performance difference are even crazier the larger the project is.


### Describe alternatives you've considered

Well, none.
The best alternative is ESLint which you already use.

The main problem I can see while implementing RSLint is writing the custom rules you wrote for ESLint again, but this time in Rust.

title: NextJS dev-mode server-side NOT recompiling and thus HMR not working in Docker
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64
      Version: Windows 10 Pro N
    Binaries:
      Node: 17.7.1
      npm: 8.5.3
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

Docker version 20.10.13, build a224086


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

N/A

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Initiating a NextJS server in dev mode in a custom ExpressJS server in Docker

### Describe the Bug

The app uses TypeScript and a custom ExpressJS server from which NextJS is initiated.

When running the server locally (no Docker) the NextJS HMR works as expected, but when inside a Docker container with the volume mapped it does not pick up the file changes.

I have confirmed that my local changes are picked up by Docker (inspecting the container after file save) via the volume mounting but the NextJS framework does not recompile the pages as expected.

Interestingly when using ts-node-dev to run launch the app, I have also had to add the `--poll` flag for the server to restart when src/server files are changes. 

### Expected Behavior

Expect to see (in the server console) the page get recompiled when saving changes locally, when the app is running inside a Docker container with the src folder volume mapped accordingly.

### To Reproduce

Clone this [nextjs-docker-hmr](https://github.com/latchy/nextjs-docker-hmr) repo and check the README for more info.  

**NB. This functionality seems to work with NextJS v9.5.3**  - this can be seen on a branch of the above repo.

body:
### Verify canary release

      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When adding a redirect with source `/about-us` and destination `/` (or any other) it redirects to `/undefined`.
This happens when i18n is enabled.

Note, that `/about-me` would work ok. The issue is occurred because `us` string after dash in `/about-us`.

### Expected Behavior

Redirects with dashed source and words that look like a locale need to redirect to proper target.

### To Reproduce

`next.config.js`

```
module.exports = async () => {
  return {
     i18n: {
      locales: [ 'en-ca', 'fr-ca' ],
      defaultLocale: 'en-ca',
    },
    async redirects() {
      return [{
        source: '/about-us',
        destination: '/',
        permanent: true,
        locale: false
      }]
    }
  }
}
```

Then `yarn dev` and try to open `/about-us` page. It redirects to `/undefined`

### Which example does this report relate to?

Any example, but I've recreated it with the blog

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

not relevant

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

not relevant 

### Describe the Bug

I'm sorry if this is a misunderstanding on my part, but I've tried this a few ways now to verify the issue.

So far I have tried, pnpm workspaces, yarn workspaces, nx, turborepo (both with default starter setup) all suffer this same issue.

The simplest example would be:

```npx create-turbo@latest```

I chose pnpm but I doubt it matters. finish setup.

Then add a single scss module with import to the index.tsx file.

```import { example } from './index.module.scss'```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1455337/159032549-9709a0b2-0b45-4af8-b9e9-07e8d2e92a0c.png)

This will lint as an error right away:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1455337/159031644-d485864b-7438-45f7-b844-6b84a371760a.png)

It will also fail the build:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1455337/159031731-b7f79c80-fa18-442b-aed5-27d1d66f4412.png)

I can get rid of the _linting_ error by installing the package "typescript-plugin-css-modules", adding a .module.scss types, and referencing them in the tsconfig file. I also needed to add to my vscode settings.json:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1455337/159032176-94630689-73fd-4049-aefb-fe8dd468c718.png)

But even when the linting is fixed, the next build still fails without adding "ignoreBuildErrors: true" to my next.config.

I've also tried installing next at the root of the project to no resolution.




### Expected Behavior

author: sneridagh

title: Retrieve the rendered route in Middleware
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Part of our company's logging standard is that we would log the matched route. This is used to group requests. For instance, as part of a structured log we would log something like:
```
{
  message: "http request"
  path: "/user/1234",
  route: "/user/[userid]"
}
```
While it is possible to retrieve the `path` in middleware, it's currently not possible to get the matched route.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Expose the matched route to middleware, preferablly on request, but on response would be acceptable. I realize this may mean compiling the routing logic and shipping to the edge to match the route there. Or I suppose it could mean returning the route as part of a response header from Next.js?

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently the only way I can think to do this is by implementing a Custom Server or by only logging `getServerSideProps()` calls. Both have significant drawbacks. Annoyingly, in `getServerSideProps()` you don't have access to the route and a Custom Server you have to call Next.js routing logic yourself. :/

title: Docs: Explain why next.lock should not be Git-ignored
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

I would like this section of the URL Imports page to be updated:
https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/url-imports#lockfile

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

The page says next.lock should not be Git-ignored, but does not go into details on why that is. I _think_ the only downside of not versioning this file is that the cache will be rebuilt on each new clone, potentially with different versions than the ones used for the last commit?

So in effect, not versioning this lock file and directory will make it so regardless of the configured caching, Next will pull the latest version of those files at the given URLs on the next clone?

I was able to test that just deleting this repository and refreshing the development mode page rebuilt it, no problem.

I intentionally want to get the latest versions of these files on each new clone, does that mean that Git-ignoring the lock directory will achieve that for me?

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/url-imports#lockfile

<sub>[DX-1663](https://linear.app/vercel/issue/DX-1663/docs-explain-why-nextlock-should-not-be-git-ignored)</sub>

author: ivanjeremic

title: Support dynamic routes in static export, with traditional SPA-like routing
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

next.js should support a static export where `getStaticPaths` is not defined but dynamic routes still work, just like in a traditional SPA: the server always serves the same index.html entrypoint, but routing is done on the client side.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Users would configure their webserver to serve `index.html` file for every path, and routing would happen just like it typically does on SPAs. on the front-end. For example:
* app contains `pages/user/[id].tsx` (user page)
* build static export, which generates `_next/static/chunks/pages/user/[id].js` and a single entry-point, `index.html`
* browser requests `GET /user/foo`, webserver hosting the static export responds with the contents of `index.html`
* the client-side code in parses the `/user/foo` route, loads `_next/static/chunks/pages/user/[id].js` and renders the user page.

Note that this already *almost* works. If you configure your webserver to serve the right files (e.g. `/users/[id.html]` for requests to `/users/<id>`) then the app works entirely as expected, including all client-side routing.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Not using next.js, but this would be a shame! Even though none of my content is static, next.js still offers a great development environment with fast TS and CSS-in-JS compilation and bundling, code splitting, and routing.

title: Next.js 12.1 SWC + Relay + Jest not transpiling `graphql` tags
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Next 12.1 

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Thank you for porting the relay-babel plugin to SWC! One issue i'm now running into is that my `graphql` tags in jest test files are not being automatically transpiled, leading to this error: 

```bash
 Invariant Violation: graphql: Unexpected invocation at runtime. Either the Babel transform was not set up, or it failed to identify this call site. Make sure it is being used verbatim as `graphql`. Note also that there cannot be a space between graphql and the backtick that follows.

       8 | const { renderWithRelay } = setupTestWrapper({
       9 |   Component: ArtistName,
    > 10 |   query: graphql`
```

### Expected Behavior

I would expect that the same transforms applied to Next js app source code would also automatically be applied to tests. 

In another post, it looks like a dev is switching back to [babel-jest](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33240#issuecomment-1035184737) in tests in order to compile these bits as a work around. 

### To Reproduce

Setup automatic next.js jest support.

Then enable Relay compiler support in Next.js config:

```
const nextConfig = {
  compiler: {
    relay: {
      src: "...",
      artifactDirectory: "...",
      language: "typescript",
    },
  },
}

module.exports = nextConfig
```

Write a test that includes the `graphql` tag, then run test.

Create an empty folder 2. Set up the following files: index.server.tsx: ```js export default function Home(props) { console.log('Rendering Home') return (
{ JSON.stringify(props) }
) } export function getStaticProps() { return { props: { heavyProps: true }} } ``` next.config.js: ```js /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ const nextConfig = { reactStrictMode: true, experimental: { runtime: 'nodejs', serverComponents: true, } } module.exports = nextConfig ``` package.json: ```json { "name": "serverside", "version": "0.1.0", "private": true, "scripts": { "dev": "next dev", "build": "next build", "start": "next start" }, "dependencies": { "next": "^12.1.1-canary.15", "react": "18.0.0-rc.2", "react-dom": "18.0.0-rc.2" } } ``` 3. Run `npm i`, `npm build` and then `npm start`. 4. Go to http://localhost:3000, and notice that the script `#__NEXT_DATA__` (in ``) has the `heavyProps: true` property. 💡 You can also notice that `Rendering Home` is only logged server-side, not client-side, which proves no rehydratation happens client-side (which is the expected behavior). Once again, there is no reason to include __NEXT_DATA__ in the bundle since no rehydratation is performed.
author: TheMrZZ

title: Request for feedback: Image Component ‘Raw’ layout
body:
Starting with version 12.1.1-canary.11, I’ve added experimental support for [a new image component mode layout mode called `“raw”`](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#layout).  This mode removes the spacers and styling associated with next/image’s responsive behavior, rendering a “raw” image element with no styling. The component still requires height and width information in order to prevent layout shift, and will pass those attributes to the underlying image unless you also provide a `sizes` attribute, which indicates that the image is expected to behave responsively.

I have also enabled the use of the `style` attribute in all image modes to pass style properties directly to the underlying image element. Be aware that in layout modes other than `”raw”`, any styling required for that layout mode will take precedence over provided styling, if there’s a conflict.

I would like to gather feedback on the new `”raw”` layout mode before we move it out of experimental. I encourage anyone who’s had trouble getting the `next/image` component styled the way they want to try the experimental `”raw”` layout and tell me how it worked. 

[See this section in the documentation for how to enable the experimental layout mode.](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#experimental-raw-layout-mode)

***

Here’s an example template for responses, though any feedback is appreciated:

1) What layout / styling behavior were you previously unable to accomplish with `next/image`?
2) What layout modes did you try?
3) Did the experimental `”raw”` layout mode solve your problem? 	- If not, what changes would allow it to solve the problem?
4) Do you have any additional feedback about the new mode or its documentation?

title: Root-level dynamic route with `fallback: 'blocking'` works differently locally than on Vercel
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.2.0: Sun Nov 28 20:29:10 PST 2021; root:xnu-8019.61.5~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8101
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.4
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

I have `rewrites` configured to proxy hits on the root `/` to my old website. 

I also have a root-level Dynamic Route at `pages/[slug]`. In the `getStaticPaths` of `slug`, I have `fallback: 'blocking'`. 

Now, when I hit `/` in local development, the request gets proxied to the old website. All good. But after I push, on Vercel `/` is no longer proxied to the old website. It seems that Vercel tries to handle the request itself, resulting in a 404.

### Expected Behavior

The behavior on my local is the desired behavior. But at least local and Vercel should be consistent.

### To Reproduce

1. Create a rewrite rule that proxies hits on the root
1. Create a root-level Dynamic Route, eg. `pages/[slug]`
1. Test locally to see if hits on the root `/` get proxied correctly
1. Push to Vercel
1. Test on Vercel to see if hits on the root get proxied correctly

author: mfix-stripe

title: Dynamic function can't import sub components.
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

Hi, there. Thanks for this amazing library and the efforts of the contributors. 💪

I can't render sub components by dynamic import. 

When I have a component like this 
```tsx
const Tabs=()=><div/>
const Tab=()=><div/>
Tabs.Tab = Tab
export default Tabs.
```

Usage
```tsx
...
const Tabs = dynamic(import('...')));
render(){
    return <Tabs>
        <Tabs.Tab></Tabs.Tab>
    </Tabs>
}
...
```
This will trigger an error, Because `Tabs.Tab` is undefined.

The relevant code of dynamic library
```
// next.js/packages/next/shared/lib/loadable.js (lines:144)
...
return React.createElement(resolve(state.loaded), props)
...
```

It looks like the mount property on the object has been removed in this step. So, I wonder to have an api that keep properties alive. 

I have seen some UI component library, according to the normal dynamic writing method, this problem still occurs, which is too vague for most people, there are some black boxes in this step, I can provide a PR if the scheme feels good, looking forward to reply.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I think it could be have an API that handles `state.loaded` data, and processing component before returning it.

e.g.
```tsx
...
const Tabs = dynamic(import('...'),{process:(loaded,components)=>{
    components.Tab = loaded.Tab; // I know the reactElement obj is immutable, Is there any other way to do it ?
    return components
}}));
...
```

### Describe alternatives you've considered

```tsx
const Tabs = dynamic(import('....'));
Tabs.Tab = dynamic(import('....').then(r=>r.default.Tab));
```

// pages/_middleware.ts

Tested on `next@latest` and `next@12.1.1-canary.16`.

```
// pages/_middleware.ts

import type { NextFetchEvent, NextRequest } from 'next/server'

export function middleware(req: NextRequest, ev: NextFetchEvent) {

```
// pages/_middleware.ts

import type { NextFetchEvent, NextRequest } from 'next/server'

title: On-demand ISR do not work correctly
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
 npx next info
/bin/sh: pnpm: command not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0
      npm: 8.3.1
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

warn  - Latest canary version not detected, detected: "12.1.0", newest: "12.1.1-canary.16".
        Please try the latest canary version (`npm install next@canary`) to confirm the issue still exists before creating a new issue.
        Read more - https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/opening-an-issue
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Vercel

### Describe the Bug

We have a NextJS front end and Sanity.io for the back end. I implement the on-demand ISR API on the `/api/revalidate` path, which is called whenever Sanity content changed. Before this, we use the time-based ISR, but I have removed that part of the code.

When deploy on Vercel, I tested by modifying the content in Sanity and it works correctly: the API is called and the page is updated, but if I make the next changes within a few minutes or so, the page won't update, even though the API is called properly in the log without catching any error. This happens most of the time, but not all the time. Sometimes, I try to trigger the API call from Insomnia, the page update again with the latest info. But most of the time, I have to wait a few mins, change the content in Sanity and `Publish` again for it to work.

If I wait for a few minutes after the first revalidation and call the API again, most of the time, the page is updated correctly. But still, not all the time. I wonder if this has anything to do with the fact that we used time-based revalidation before. Maybe some caching laying around, not sure.

On an addition note, when I test the revalidate API call from Insomnia, for both local prod build or Vercel deployed build, sometimes, it takes a whole minute to resolve the query. So one of the possibility is that Vercel has a timeout config for a function call.

Also another quick note that might help others: we use [Next middleware auth](https://github.com/vercel/examples/blob/main/edge-functions/basic-auth-password/pages/_middleware.ts) to block people from visiting our development page, when I call the `revalidation` API, it is blocked by the popup therefore only always throw errors. The error message was only `Failed to revalidate ${urlPath}` and I was only able to fix it by deep-dive into the function. Maybe throwing the error with the actual result of the `fetch` instead would be more helpful? :)

Thank you!

### Expected Behavior

Whenever the `/api/revalidate` API call, force update the page content

### To Reproduce

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14198542/159480104-d252a71c-3093-4a1a-862a-75dc498a63fd.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/14198542/159480461-68a13a29-453c-45d0-a65e-032acc84111e.png)

body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

      Platform: win32
      Arch: x64

    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Irrelevant, the issue stems from the OS/NodeJS server

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Irrelevant, the issue only occurs in development mode

### Describe the Bug

On windows, drive letter capitalization causes Next to throw errors like this when running `next dev`:

> warn  - Invalid casing detected for project dir, received [c:\projects\website]() actual path [C:\projects\website](), see more info here [https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/invalid-project-dir-casing]()

This occurs both in existing projects upgraded from Next 11 to Next 12, as well as new Next 12 projects. The error only occurs on Windows - if I run a WSL (Windows subsystem for Linux) the error disappears because Linux is not case sensitive.

This was a minor problem turning major now, as it prohibits developers on windows machines to upgrade Next to latest.

See this discussion for further proof:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33299

### Expected Behavior

Next 12 is expected to run without errors on Windows, just like Next 11, without regards for drive letter capitalization.

This is uncircumventable on Windows, because it is not possible to change the drive letter capitalization, so the issue must be fixed in the dependency that throws this warning.

### To Reproduce

- Install Next 12 on a Windows machine. It seems to happen on multiple node/NPM versions.
- Run Next in development mode, `npm run dev`
- Observe the terminal output.

title: Don't override user-agent in fetch API in Next.js middleware
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Debian 5.10.103-1 (2022-03-07)
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.18.1
      npm: 6.14.15
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: N/A
      react-dom: N/A
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When doing fetch, we manually pass headers including `user-agent` into our `fetch` API call.
However, this user-agent we set is specifically overridden by Next.js.

I believe the issue is this line:
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/server/web/sandbox/context.ts#L157

A quick fix would be:
```ts
if (!init.headers.has('user-agent')) {
  init.headers.set('user-agent', 'Next.js Middleware')
}
```

Alternatively, if you're keen on having your user-agent regardless, `Next.js Middleware` could be added as `x-user-agent` either in general or just when `user-agent` is already specified.

```ts
init.headers.set('x-user-agent', 'Next.js Middleware')
```

```ts
init.headers.set((!init.headers.has('user-agent')) ? 'user-agent' : 'x-user-agent', 'Next.js Middleware')
```

### Expected Behavior

If no user-agent is specified, add the `Next.js Middleware` user-agent.
If a user-agent is specified, don't override/remove it.

### To Reproduce

```ts
const apiResponse = await fetch('https://example.org', {
  headers: {
    'user-agent': request?.ua.ua
  },
  keepalive: true
}).catch((error) => { console.error('RIP'); });
```

title: Replace react-router-bootstrap?

### Describe the feature you'd like to request

I've used [react-router-bootstrap](https://www.npmjs.com/package/react-router-bootstrap) to bring React Router features to React Bootstrap. This lets me wrap any linking object with `<LinkContainer>` and the link will function like a React Router link by not reloading all assets. next/router does similar with `<Link>`, but that doesn't properly adapt Bootstrap components like react-router-bootstrap does.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like Next.js to either automatically work with React Bootstrap, offer a component similar to `<LinkContainer>`, or make the `<Link>` component properly handle Bootstrap linking components.

- Ditching Next.js.

title: Next.js build cache isn't properly invalidated when there are changes outside the project
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

Output of `next info`

```
/bin/sh: pnpm: command not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.1
      react-dom: 17.0.1
```

I can also confirm this issue happens in versions `12.0.2` and `12.1.0`.

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 99.0.4844.74

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

`next start`, `next dev`, and Vercel

### Describe the Bug

Unless the `.next` cache folder is deleted (i.e., `rm -rf .next`) changes made to files outside the Next.js project aren't picked up by `next dev` or `next build`.

We first ran into this issue ~1 month ago in local development. After finding https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/33864 I left a comment on the discussion that issue was moved to: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33929#discussioncomment-2295926

Today, after a deployment to Vercel wasn't working, I did some more investigation on this issue. I realized that this wasn't just the case for `next dev` but also for `next build` and the changes we had made weren't being reflected in our new deployment since Vercel caches `.next` in its build cache.

It's worth mentioning that we're not modifying `node_modules` directly, and instead are simply using a monorepo with Yarn workspaces. Next.js just isn't picking up these changes and the stale `.next` cache is being used.

### Expected Behavior

If _any_ dependent files change (not just in the Next.js project) the `.next` cache shouldn't be used.

### To Reproduce

I have attempted to reproduce this in a fresh Next.js application, but unfortunately the issue doesn't reproduce. I will continue to attempt to reproduce and will update the issue if I find a reproduction.

title: Prop mismatch, server: "null", client: "true" for a value computed by useState(false)
body:
### Verify canary release - [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release ### Provide environment information ``` Operating System: Platform: linux Arch: x64 Version: #1 SMP PREEMPT Mon, 21 Mar 2022 22:59:40 +0000 Binaries: Node: 17.7.2 npm: 8.5.5 Yarn: 1.22.18 pnpm: N/A Relevant packages: next: 12.1.1-canary.17 react: 17.0.2 react-dom: 17.0.2 ``` ### What browser are you using? (if relevant) Firefox 98.0.2; Firefox developer edition 99.0b7 ### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant) next dev ### Describe the Bug A page component renders two buttons, which are disabled depending on state of useState. Changing that state, and refreshing the page results in a ```Warning: Prop `disabled` did not match. Server: "null" Client: "true"```. Neither button is disabled, but one of them should be. If I only render one button, the error doesn't happen anymore even after changing state and refreshing. This only seems to happen on firefox, tested in chromium and it doesn't seem to happen. I found [this discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/21999), but no issue. The discussion seems to tend towards this being a firefox bug, but since it no longer happens once we go from two buttons to one, I figured I should file an issue. ### Expected Behavior There should be no mismatch, there's no difference between the server and client. ### To Reproduce ```ts import type { NextPage } from 'next' import { useState } from 'react' const Home: NextPage = () => { const [isDisabled, setDisabled] = useState(false) return (
) } export default Home ``` In firefox, click the button to change default state, refresh and it should result in ```Warning: Prop `disabled` did not match. Server: "null" Client: "true"```. Comment out the second button, refresh, change default state and refresh again and there's no mismatch anymore.

title: Middleware does not take rewrites into account
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #40-Ubuntu SMP Mon Mar 7 08:03:10 UTC 2022
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.0
      npm: 6.14.13
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

next start

### Describe the Bug

When using rewrites, the middleware dedicated to the destination page is not executed: the middleware dedicated to the source page is executed instead, if it exists.

```
/pages
├ ...
├ /news
| ├ _middelware
| └ [...slug]
├ /blog
| └ _middelware
├ ...
```
And in next.config.js:
```js
  // ...
  async rewrites() {
    return [
      {
        source: '/blog/:slug*',
        destination: '/news/:slug*',
      },
    ]
  },
  // ...
```
In this case, visiting `/blog/something` display the `/news/[...slug]` page with the `{ slug: 'something' }` query, but the associated `/news/_middleware` is not executed. Instead the `/blog/_middleware` (associated with no pages: `/pages/blog` does not contain any) is executed.

### Expected Behavior

I expect the _middleware associated with the rewrite destination page to be executed and not the one associated with a hypothetical source page.

### To Reproduce

Here is a minimal reproduction: https://github.com/cvolant/next-middleware-rewrite-issue

Reproduction steps are described in the readme file.

### Provide environment information

Operating System:

    Binaries:
      Node: 16.14.0

      Yarn: 1.22.18
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2


### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 99.0.4844.83 (Official) (x86_64)

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

Not relevant

### Describe the Bug

I think that the reproduction repository I have prepared fully describes the bug so please have a look ⬇️ .

* If we change the description prop in `lowercasedEnhancePropsFunction` function HMR will properly refresh the component with the updated data.

* But If we change the title prop in `CapitalizedEnhancePropsFunction` function then nothing will change in our component.

* Issue occurs **only** if we have a function with **capitalized name**, imported from another file and that's the **only one** export existing in this file. If we export anything else from this file (i.e. export const hack = undefined) HMR will work properly.

### Expected Behavior

HMR should refresh the component with the updated data every time, no matter the name of the function we are importing.

### To Reproduce

I have created a reproduction repository:

> https://github.com/usertive/next-js-issue

1. yarn dev
2. Change description in `lowercasedEnhancePropsFunction` function - works.
3. Change title in `CapitalizedEnhancePropsFunction` function - doesn't work.

<sub>[NEXT-369](https://linear.app/vercel/issue/NEXT-369/fast-refresh-doesnt-work-when-importing-function-with-capitalized-name)</sub>

title: Next (canary) does not support the upcoming TypeScript 4.7 NodeNext/Node12 moduleResolution
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information


    Operating System:
      Platform: linux
      Arch: x64
      Version: #1 SMP Mon Nov 8 10:21:19 UTC 2021
    Binaries:
      Node: 17.6.0
      npm: 8.5.1
      Yarn: N/A
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

TypeScript have [added support](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/33079#issuecomment-1043271180) for package exports in the upcoming 1.4.7 release. This has included releasing a new module option called [node12](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/46452). However in order to use this feature (and upcoming ESM features) `moduleResolution` needs to be set to `NodeNext` or `Node12`. 

This is not possible to do in a next project, because Next changes it back to `node` at build time. Meaning users of Next are not able to use TypeScript's upcoming features.

NextJS Output:
```
We detected TypeScript in your project and reconfigured your tsconfig.json file for you. Strict-mode is set to false by default.

The following mandatory changes were made to your tsconfig.json:

        - moduleResolution was set to node (to match webpack resolution)
```

### Expected Behavior

Node12 and NodeNext to be supported in Next so I can make use of [package exports](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#exports) in my other dependencies. 

### To Reproduce

- Add `"typescript": "4.7.0-dev.20220322"` to your package.json assuming you're already using typescript.
- Set `moduleResolution` to `Node12` or `NodeNext`

body:
### Verify canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Nov 10 22:23:07 PST 2021; root:xnu-7195.141.14~1/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.19.0
      npm: 6.14.16
      Yarn: 1.22.15
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

vercel

### Describe the Bug

The below example will return a valid response when deployed to Vercel.
But when running locally you will get `[object Object]`.
You may want to convert a response into a `NextResponse` in order to mutate headers, and access builtin `NextResponse` methods like `nextUrl` or `.cookie()`.

```javascript
async function middleware() {
    const response = await fetch('https://www.vercel.com');
    const clonedResponse = new NextResponse(response.body, response);
    return clonedResponse;
}
```

Just using native `Response` works in both environments.
```javascript
async function middleware() {
    const response = await fetch('https://www.vercel.com');
    const clonedResponse = new Response(response.body, response);
    return clonedResponse;
}
```

### Expected Behavior

I should be able to convert a `Response` into a `NextResponse` in both local dev AND when deployed to vercel.

### To Reproduce

See code snippet above.

title: Module resolution is inconsistent between browser and server builds
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

```
/bin/sh: pnpm: command not found

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: arm64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.17
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.17
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2
```

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

Chrome 99.0.4844.74

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

`next dev`

### Describe the Bug

When creating the browser and server bundles, Next.js handles module resolution differently. Specifically, when bundling for Node.js, Next.js will use the `exports` field in `package.json` but not the `module` field. When bundling for the browser, it will support both `exports` and `module`.

This bug results in there being two instances of a peer dependency in the server build only (i.e., not in the client build). This causes issues with React hooks, for example.

### Expected Behavior

The expected behavior is that both bundles are consistent.

### To Reproduce

```
git clone https://github.com/migueloller/next-module-bug
cd next-module-bug
yarn
yarn workspace app dev // visit http://localhost:3000 and compare logs between browser and server
```

Browser logs should show:
```
{ moduleLib: 'esm', exportsLib: 'esm' }
```

Server logs should show:
```
{ moduleLib: 'cjs', exportsLib: 'esm' }
```
author: migueloller

title: Docs: Official Documentation For Custom Fonts
body:
### What is the improvement or update you wish to see?

Official documentation on how to deal with custom fonts should be listed within the Font Optimization page at the very top and it should very clearly explain how I can best use custom fonts on my Next.js application.

### Is there any context that might help us understand?

Here's the journey for me, today:

I google: "Next.js custom font" and am given this as the top result:
https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/font-optimization

I happen to know that this only works for Google fonts, but somebody else might not. They'll be very confused until they scroll _very_ far down the page to see a tiny aside listed at the bottom of the page that says:

> Note: Font Optimization does not currently support self-hosted fonts.

So, self-hosted font loading strategies are not described anywhere in the Next.js documentation leaving self-hosted font users floundering around the web.

Now what?

- Searching issues yields a lot of irrelevant or unanswered tickets.
- [In discussions (with the search: "self-hosted font")](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions?discussions_q=self-hosted+font)
- [In discussions (with the search: "custom font")](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions?discussions_q=custom+font)
- [This popular stack overflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61909298/how-to-use-self-hosted-fonts-face-using-nextjs)

Somewhere along this wild ride, I'm directed to this fabulous website from @leerob: [https://leerob.io/blog/fonts](https://leerob.io/blog/fonts)

I'm on the hunt to remove layout shift, so - after reading this article - I am preloading all my font assets from the public folder in `_document` AND I'm using `font-display: optional` as outlined. All seems sorted, but once I deploy my build, I see that - very often - the custom font is never used. It just stays as Arial.

In devtools, I see the following error:

```
The resource <URL> was preloaded using link preload but not used within a few seconds from the window's load event. Please make sure it has an appropriate `as` value and it is preloaded intentionally.
website.com/:1 The resource https://website.com/public/custom-font.woff2 was preloaded using link preload but not used within a few seconds from the window's load event. Please make sure it has an appropriate `as` value and it is preloaded intentionally.
```

This leads me to [a Next.js issue which dismisses the warning](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/6517#issuecomment-469063892) and 2 popular Stack overflow issues that don't have a solution [[1](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/49674092/preloading-font-with-rel-preload) and [2](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55819473/chrome-unused-preload-warning-for-an-icon-font-that-is-used)]

I spent a lot of time on it and eventually landed on [this as the solution](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35582#issuecomment-1085259042)

### Does the docs page already exist? Please link to it.

_No response_

title: ESLint @next/next/inline-script-id rule throws error when using spread props on next/script component
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information


    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.3.0: Wed Jan  5 21:37:58 PST 2022; root:xnu-8019.80.24~20/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.19.0
      npm: 6.14.16
      Yarn: 1.22.10
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.0
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When wrapping the `Script` component in another component and spreading the props into `Script`, the Next ESLint rule for inline scripts throws an error related to iterating over the spread props. For example:

```jsx
import Script from "next/script";

function MyScript(props) {
  return (
    // ESLint passes when removing {...props}, otherwise errors on rule @next/next/inline-script-id
    <Script id="my-script" strategy="afterInteractive" {...props}>
      {`console.log("hello world");`}
    </Script>
  );
}
```

```sh
> next lint

Cannot read property 'forEach' of undefined
Occurred while linting /Users/erijung1/Projects/temp/codesandbox-next-script-issue/pages/index.js:6
Rule: "@next/next/inline-script-id"
npm ERR! code ELIFECYCLE
npm ERR! errno 1
npm ERR! nextjs@1.0.0 lint: `next lint`
npm ERR! Exit status 1
npm ERR! 
npm ERR! Failed at the nextjs@1.0.0 lint script.
npm ERR! This is probably not a problem with npm. There is likely additional logging output above.
```

### Expected Behavior

The ESLint rule should not error when spread props are used on `Script`.

### To Reproduce

Repository: https://github.com/erikjung/codesandbox-next-script-issue

- `npm install`
- `npm run lint`
- Observe error from ESLint

To verify:
- Remove `{...props}` from `MyScript`. 
- `npm run lint`

title: `npm run build` sometimes times out building static pages in Next 12 for our repository
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 20.6.0: Wed Jan 12 22:22:42 PST 2022; root:xnu-7195.141.19~2/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 16.13.1
      npm: 8.1.2
      Yarn: 1.22.4
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1-canary.18
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

Various pages in our app often time out during static page generation when using Next 12. We first noticed this in our Vercel builds, where they time out occasionally, but have recently been able to reproduce this fairly reliably on the command line.

Using node 17 seems to make this worse, but it does happen fairly reliably on 16 as well. Different pages time out in different builds, ie build 1:

and sometimes they don't time out at all:
```

├ ○ /u/[handle]/[workspace]/[name]         6.71 kB         701 kB
└ ○ /u/[handle]/message                    1.16 kB         616 kB

```

We've tried to verify that there aren't network requests happening, and indeed this seems to happen even if we strip the `_app_` and `_document` files out. 

As far as we can tell this starts happening when we include some of our dependencies in a file, and is not dependent on any particular component/application structure.

### Expected Behavior

We expect pages to take roughly the same amount of time to build across different runs as they did in Next 11.

Downgrading to Next 11.1 does seem to fix this, but strangely enough adding a `.babelrc` to disable SWC builds does not seem to.

### To Reproduce

```
git clone https://github.com/mysilio-co/garden.git
cd garden
git checkout nextjs-bugreport
npm install
npm run build
```

You may need to run `npm run build` more than once because unfortunately the results seem to be somewhat nondeterministic - the first time I ran this it completed with:

```
info  - Creating an optimized production build
info  - Compiled successfully
info  - Collecting page data
info  - Generating static pages (14/14)

title: Emotion SWC Plugin - Spaces between expressions are being stripped
body:
### Verify canary release

- [X] I verified that the issue exists in Next.js canary release

### Provide environment information

    Operating System:
      Platform: darwin
      Arch: x64
      Version: Darwin Kernel Version 21.4.0: Mon Feb 21 20:34:37 PST 2022; root:xnu-8020.101.4~2/RELEASE_X86_64
    Binaries:
      Node: 14.17.0
      npm: 6.14.13
      Yarn: 1.19.1
      pnpm: N/A
    Relevant packages:
      next: 12.1.1
      react: 17.0.2
      react-dom: 17.0.2

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

When using Emotion with SWC, spaces between template strings expressions are being stripped. This can cause invalid CSS to be generated.

### Expected Behavior

There should be spaces preserved between expressions, to ensure that valid CSS is generated

### To Reproduce

## Input
```tsx
const unitNormal = '1rem';
const unitLarge = '2rem';

const Example = styled.div`
  margin: ${unitNormal} ${unitLarge};
`;
```

## Output
```tsx
var unitNormal = '1rem';
var unitLarge = '2rem';
var Example = /*#__PURE__*/ (0,_emotion_styled__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__["default"])("div", {
    target: "e1s94g990",
    label: "Example"
})("margin:", unitNormal, unitLarge, ";");
```

This results in the following CSS:
```css
.next-19a10xq-Example {
    margin: 1rem2rem;
}
```

author: HarrisonHemstreet

title: Allow configuration of cache dir
body:
### Describe the feature you'd like to request

We should be able to configure the location of the cache directory. Currently it's `.next` of the root of the project, but in several situations where the filesystem is read-only (GCP, Nix, CI), this means that the project cannot be built and run.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Either some configuration in the config file or read from an environment variable.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Working around this is difficult, so a more direct supported solution would be preferable.

### What browser are you using? (if relevant)

_No response_

### How are you deploying your application? (if relevant)

_No response_

### Describe the Bug

With a page structure like:
```
// pages/index.js
// pages/[customer]/_middleware.js
// pages/[customer]/dashboard.js
```
And a `pages/[customer]/_middleware` file containing:
```tsx
export function middleware(req: NextRequest) {
  console.log(req.url)
}
```

I would expect `_middleware` to only apply to pages that resolve within that subdirectory, however it gets applied to every request:
```
wait  - compiling /[team]/_middleware...
event - compiled client and server successfully in 114 ms (765 modules)
http://localhost:3000/api/auth/session
http://localhost:3000/api/session
wait  - compiling /api/auth/[...nextauth]...
wait  - compiling /api/session...
event - compiled client and server successfully in 86 ms (779 modules)
http://localhost:3000/customer/1/dashboard
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/chunks/main.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/chunks/webpack.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/chunks/react-refresh.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/chunks/pages/_app.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/development/_buildManifest.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/development/_ssgManifest.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/chunks/pages/_error.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/_next/static/development/_middlewareManifest.js?ts=1648344461416
http://localhost:3000/api/session
http://localhost:3000/api/auth/session
http://localhost:3000/favicon.ico
```

### Expected Behavior

I would expect the middleware not to be invoked on an unmatched route.

### To Reproduce

See above description with a trivial example.
